# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a biomedical instrumentation system. List its main functional blocks.**

**Model Answer:**

A **biomedical instrumentation system** is an engineered device that measures, monitors, or controls a physiological variable of the human body by converting it into an electrical signal for analysis, display, or recording.

**Main functional blocks:**

1. **Patient / source** of the physiological variable.
2. **Transducer / sensor** (converts physiological quantity → electrical signal).
3. **Signal conditioning** (amplification + filtering + isolation).
4. **Signal processing & display** (DSP + monitor/recorder).
5. **Power supply with patient isolation** (safety module).
6. **Calibration unit** (for instrument verification).

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for block listing)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Differentiate between a transducer and an electrode. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Transducer | Electrode |
|-----------|------------|-----------|
| **Definition** | A general device that converts one form of energy into another | A specialized transducer that converts ionic current in the body into electronic current in the instrument |
| **Scope** | Broad — measures any physical/chemical/biological variable | Narrow — specifically used for biopotential measurement (ECG, EEG, EMG) |
| **Example** | Thermistor (for body temperature) | Ag/AgCl surface electrode (for ECG) |
| **Operating Range** | Varies by sensor (mV to V) | µV to mV (low-level biopotentials) |

*(3 marks — table format is best)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Either Q(A) or Q(B) must be answered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the various functional blocks of a biomedical instrumentation system. Describe the role of each block. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **classification of biomedical instruments** with suitable examples. Compare diagnostic, therapeutic, and monitoring instruments. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

A complete biomedical instrumentation system consists of the following functional blocks (refer to **Section 4.1 diagram** of these notes):

1. **Patient / Living System (Source):** Provides the physiological variable. Example: human heart producing electrical activity.

2. **Transducer / Sensor:** Converts the physiological quantity into a proportional electrical signal. Example: ECG electrode, piezoelectric crystal in ultrasound.

3. **Preamplifier:** A low-noise, high-gain amplifier (typically an instrumentation amplifier like INA128) that boosts the micro-volt-level bio-signal to a workable voltage level.

4. **Filter Network:** Composed of a **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** to remove baseline wander, a **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** to remove high-frequency noise, and a **Notch Filter (50/60 Hz)** to remove power-line interference.

5. **Signal Conditioning Stage:** Provides isolation (using opto-couplers), level shifting, and linearization.

6. **A/D Converter (ADC):** Digitizes the analog bio-signal for further processing. Resolution typically **10–16 bits** at sampling rates from **250 Hz (ECG)** to **20 kHz (EMG)**.

7. **Microprocessor / DSP Block:** Performs digital signal processing — averaging, FFT, feature extraction, compression.

8. **Display / Recorder / Alarm / Storage:** The output subsystem — LCD/CRT monitor, thermal printer, alarm buzzer, and memory (RAM/SD card).

9. **Power Supply with Isolation:** Provides regulated DC power with patient-isolation (limits leakage current to < **10 µA** per IEC 60601-1).

10. **Calibration Source:** A built-in reference signal generator (e.g., 1 mV square pulse) used to verify the instrument's gain and linearity periodically.

*(7 marks — full marks awarded for neat diagram + role of each block)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Classification of Biomedical Instruments:**

**(i) Diagnostic Instruments** — used to *identify* a disease or condition.
- Examples: ECG machine, EEG machine, X-ray, CT scanner, MRI, ultrasound scanner, spirometer.
- Purpose: Acquire and display a physiological signal/image for the clinician's interpretation.

**(ii) Therapeutic Instruments** — used to *treat* a disease or maintain a physiological function.
- Examples: Pacemaker, defibrillator, ventilator, dialysis machine, infusion pump, surgical diathermy.
- Purpose: Deliver a controlled energy (electrical, mechanical, or chemical) to the patient to correct a physiological dysfunction.

**(iii) Monitoring Instruments** — used to *continuously track* vital parameters.
- Examples: ICU patient monitor, pulse oximeter, fetal heart monitor, blood-pressure Holter.
- Purpose: Provide real-time display and alarm for one or more vital signs.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Diagnostic | Therapeutic | Monitoring |
|--------|------------|-------------|------------|
| **Primary Action** | Measures & displays | Delivers therapy | Tracks continuously |
| **Output** | Waveform / image | Energy / drug | Real-time values + alarm |
| **Power Direction** | Patient → Instrument | Instrument → Patient | Patient → Instrument |
| **Example** | ECG | Defibrillator | Pulse Oximeter |

*(7 marks — classification + comparison table)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **various physiological signals** of the human body. Give their amplitude and frequency range. (7 marks)

**(b)** A thermistor transducer is used to measure body temperature. If the output changes from **2.5 V at 35 °C** to **3.5 V at 40 °C**, determine the **sensitivity** in mV/°C. Also explain the role of the transducer in a biomedical system. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

The human body produces several **electrical and non-electrical signals** that can be measured. The major biopotentials are:

| Biopotential | Source | Amplitude | Frequency Range | Instrument |
|--------------|--------|-----------|-----------------|------------|
| **ECG** | Heart | **0.5 – 5 mV** | **0.05 – 150 Hz** | Electrocardiograph |
| **EEG** | Brain | **5 – 300 µV** | **0.5 – 100 Hz** | Electroencephalograph |
| **EMG** | Skeletal muscle | **50 µV – 5 mV** | **20 – 5000 Hz** | Electromyograph |
| **EOG** | Eye | **10 µV – 5 mV** | **DC – 50 Hz** | Eye-movement recorder |
| **ERG** | Retina | **5 µV – 1 mV** | **0.2 – 200 Hz** | Retinograph |
| **GSR** | Skin | **1 – 50 µS** | **0.05 – 0.3 Hz** | Polygraph |

**Description of each:**

- **ECG:** Records the electrical activity of the heart during depolarization and repolarization. P-wave, QRS-complex, and T-wave are the main features. Used to detect arrhythmias, myocardial infarction, and conduction blocks.

- **EEG:** Records summed synaptic activity of cortical neurons. Used to diagnose epilepsy, sleep disorders, encephalopathies, and confirm brain death.

- **EMG:** Records the electrical activity of skeletal muscles during contraction. Used in neuromuscular disease diagnosis (myasthenia gravis, muscular dystrophy).

- **EOG:** Measures the steady corneal-retinal potential (≈ 0.4–1.0 mV) used to track eye movement. Used in sleep studies (REM detection) and ophthalmology.

- **ERG:** Records retinal response to light stimulation. Used in diagnosing retinal degenerative diseases (retinitis pigmentosa).

- **GSR (EDA):** Measures skin conductance variations due to sweat-gland activity. Used in lie-detector (polygraph) and emotion/stress monitoring.

*(7 marks — full table + 2–3 lines per signal)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**

$$V_1 = 2.5 \text{ V at } T_1 = 35 \degree \text{C}$$
$$V_2 = 3.5 \text{ V at } T_2 = 40 \degree \text{C}$$

**Step 1 — Sensitivity Formula**

$$S = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta T}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta V$ and $\Delta T$**

$$\Delta V = 3.5 - 2.5 = 1.0 \text{ V}$$
$$\Delta T = 40 - 35 = 5 \degree \text{C}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$S = \frac{1.0 \text{ V}}{5 \degree \text{C}} = 0.2 \text{ V/°C}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mV/°C**

$$S = 200 \text{ mV/°C}$$

**Final Answer:** The sensitivity of the thermistor transducer is **$S = 200 \ \text{mV/°C}$**.

**Role of the Transducer in a Biomedical System:**

The transducer is the **front-end sensor** of any biomedical instrument. Its role is to:

1. **Interface the biological system to the electronic system** — convert non-electrical physiological signals (heat, pressure, flow, chemical concentration) into measurable electrical signals.
2. **Provide selectivity** — respond to the specific variable of interest while rejecting others.
3. **Define accuracy and resolution** — the overall system accuracy is fundamentally limited by the transducer's linearity, hysteresis, and drift.
4. **Enable patient safety** — when designed as an isolated, biocompatible sensor, the transducer allows measurement without harming the patient.

In the context of body-temperature measurement, the thermistor is the transducer. It is followed by a **signal conditioner** (Wheatstone bridge + instrumentation amplifier), a **filter**, an **ADC**, and finally a **display**.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for numerical + 4 marks for descriptive part)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "transducer" with "electrode".** A transducer is a general term; an electrode is a specific type of transducer used for biopotentials.
> 2. **Always include units** in the final numerical answer (mV/°C, not just a number).
> 3. **Block diagrams must be drawn with arrows** showing signal flow direction — students often forget arrows and lose **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Patient safety (isolation, leakage current)** is a frequently-asked concept — omitting it costs marks.
> 5. **State the formula before substituting values** — examiners reward the *method*, not just the *answer*.
> 6. **Avoid over-simplified definitions** like "an instrument used in hospitals" — examiners expect *technical* definitions involving signal conversion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

- [ ] **Biomedical Instrumentation** = science of measuring biological variables using electronic systems.
- [ ] **Five sub-systems:** Measurement, Signal Conditioning, Display/Processing, Power & Safety, Auxiliary.
- [ ] **Transducer** = energy converter (physiological → electrical).
- [ ] **Electrode** = specialized transducer for bio-electric signals.
- [ ] **Sensitivity** $S = \Delta V_{\text{out}} / \Delta x_{\text{in}}$ (e.g., mV/°C, V/mmHg).
- [ ] **Gain in dB** $= 20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{out}} / V_{\text{in}})$.
- [ ] **Nernst Equation** $= E_0 - 61.5 \log_{10}(a_{\text{red}}/a_{\text{ox}})$ mV at 37 °C.
- [ ] **ADC Resolution** $Q = V_{\text{FS}} / 2^N$.
- [ ] **Nyquist Sampling** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$.
- [ ] **Patient Safety:** Leakage current < **10 µA** (Type CF, IEC 60601-1).
- [ ] **ECG amplitude:** 0.5 – 5 mV; **EEG amplitude:** 5 – 300 µV; **EMG amplitude:** 50 µV – 5 mV.
- [ ] **Classification:** Diagnostic / Therapeutic / Monitoring / Rehabilitative / Imaging.
- [ ] **Instrumentation amplifier** is the standard first stage of every bio-signal acquisition system.
- [ ] **Three domains** intersect: Electrical + Mechanical + Computer Science (interdisciplinary nature).
- [ ] **Calibration** is essential before every clinical use of the instrument.

---

**End of Module 1 — Topic: Introduction to Biomedical Instrumentation System**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Physiological Systems - Heart, Lungs and Muscles. Sources of Bioelectric Potential - Resting and Action Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET612 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Physiological Systems - Heart, Lungs and Muscles. Sources of Bioelectric Potential - Resting and Action Potential

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Physiological System?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Knutson, 2002; KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A *physiological system* is an organised assembly of organs, tissues, cells and extra-cellular fluids that jointly perform one or more vital life-sustaining functions through coordinated biochemical, mechanical and electrical activity. In the context of biomedical engineering, the three most clinically and instrumentally relevant systems are the **cardiovascular** (heart and vessels), the **respiratory** (lungs and airways), and the **musculoskeletal** (skeletal, smooth and cardiac muscle) systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Tag:** This topic directly maps to **CO1** — *“Describe the basic physiological systems of the human body and the origin of bioelectric potentials.”*

### Conceptual Analogy — The City Power Grid

Imagine a **city** that must remain alive 24×7:
- The **heart** is the *electrical pump station* that pushes a fluid (blood) through a closed pipe network.
- The **lungs** are the *gas exchange plant* that refuels the blood with oxygen and removes carbon-dioxide exhaust.
- The **muscles** are the *motorised actuators* that convert the chemical fuel (ATP) into mechanical motion.
- **Bioelectric potentials** are the *control signals* (action potentials) flowing along the wiring (nerves) that coordinate every component.

This unified analogy will be used throughout the notes to make every concept sticky and intuitive.

---

## 1.2 The Heart — Anatomy in 60 Seconds

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The **human heart** is a four-chambered, dual-pump, striated muscular organ that generates the **cardiac cycle** — a synchronised sequence of electrical excitation, mechanical contraction, valve actuation and blood ejection.

| Feature | Engineering Equivalent | Value (Adult, Rest) |
|---|---|---|
| Resting heart rate (HR) | Pump cycle frequency | **60–100 bpm** (≈ **1.0–1.7 Hz**) |
| Stroke volume (SV) | Volume per cycle | **≈ 70 mL / beat** |
| Cardiac output (CO) | Pump throughput | **≈ 5 L/min** |
| Conduction velocity (Purkinje) | Signal propagation speed | **≈ 2–4 m/s** |
| AV-node delay | Synchronisation buffer | **≈ 100 ms** |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** The heart is essentially a *self-excited, two-stage positive-displacement pump* with built-in delay lines (AV node) to ensure atrial contraction always precedes ventricular contraction.

---

## 1.3 The Lungs — Anatomy in 60 Seconds

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The **lungs** are a paired, elastic, alveolar organ system whose function is **external respiration** — the bulk transfer of O₂ from inspired air to pulmonary capillary blood and the simultaneous transfer of CO₂ in the opposite direction, driven by negative-pressure ventilation.

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Trachea → Bronchi → Bronchioles | Conduction zone (no gas exchange) |
| Alveoli (≈ 300–500 million) | **Respiratory zone**; surface area ≈ **70 m²** |
| Diaphragm | Primary inspiratory muscle |
| Pleural cavity | Negative-pressure coupling |

**Lung Volumes (Standard Adult Male, BTPS conditions):**

| Symbol | Volume | Typical Value (mL) |
|---|---|---|
| TV (Tidal Volume) | Normal quiet breath | **500** |
| IRV (Inspiratory Reserve) | Max extra inhalation | **3000** |
| ERV (Expiratory Reserve) | Max extra exhalation | **1100** |
| RV (Residual Volume) | Air after max expiration | **1200** |
| **VC** = TV + IRV + ERV | Vital Capacity | **≈ 4600** |
| **TLC** = VC + RV | Total Lung Capacity | **≈ 5800** |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** Think of the lungs as a *two-balloon assembly* inside a sealed rigid box (thorax). When the diaphragm pulls the floor of the box downward, box volume ↑, pressure ↓ (Boyle’s law), and atmospheric air rushes in.

---

## 1.4 The Muscles — Anatomy in 60 Seconds

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** **Muscle** is a contractile tissue specialised to convert the chemical energy of ATP into mechanical force and motion. Three histological types exist: **skeletal** (voluntary, striated), **cardiac** (involuntary, striated, auto-rhythmic) and **smooth** (involuntary, non-striated).

| Property | Skeletal | Cardiac | Smooth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Striations | Yes | Yes | No |
| Innervation | Somatic | Autonomic | Autonomic |
| Contraction control | Voluntary | Involuntary | Involuntary |
| Action potential duration | 1–2 ms | **200–300 ms** | Variable |
| Function | Posture, locomotion | Blood pumping | Vessel/viscera tone |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** A **muscle fibre** behaves like a *tensile cable* whose length is actively controlled by a ratchet of interdigitating filaments (actin & myosin) powered by ATP hydrolysis.

---

## 1.5 Sources of Bioelectric Potential

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **bioelectric potential** is an electrical potential difference (measured in volts) that arises across a biological membrane due to (i) selective ionic permeability of the lipid bilayer and (ii) active ion-transport pumps (e.g. Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase). It is the *biophysical foundation* of nerve conduction, muscle contraction, cardiac rhythm and virtually all electrophysiological measurements (ECG, EMG, EEG, EOG).

**Two fundamental potentials:**

1. **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** — The steady-state, near-constant voltage (≈ **−70 mV** in a typical neuron, ≈ **−90 mV** in ventricular myocyte) when the cell is not being stimulated.
2. **Action Potential (AP)** — A rapid, self-propagating, all-or-none transient depolarisation-repolarisation wave that travels along the membrane, with peak overshoot of ≈ **+30 to +40 mV** and total duration of **1–2 ms (nerve) to 200–300 ms (cardiac ventricle)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** The cell membrane is a *leaky battery* — at rest it holds a stable −70 mV charge (resting potential); when a stimulus pushes it past a *threshold* (≈ −55 mV), voltage-gated Na⁺ channels pop open en masse and the cell briefly inverts to +30 mV (action potential), then resets.

### Key Constants (Bolded for Revision)

| Constant | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **R** (Universal Gas Constant) | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Thermodynamic constant |
| **F** (Faraday’s Constant) | **96 485 C/mol** | Charge per mole of electrons |
| **T** (Body Temperature) | **310 K (37 °C)** | Absolute body temperature |
| **RT/F** at 310 K | **≈ 26.71 mV** | Base Nernst multiplier |
| **(2.303 RT/F)** at 310 K | **≈ 61.5 mV** | Log₁₀ form multiplier |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Heart — Conduction System & Cardiac Cycle

### 2.1.1 Electrical Conduction Pathway

The heart possesses its own **specialised conduction system** (auto-rhythmic, no external nerve needed for initiation):

1. **Sino-Atrial (SA) Node** — Pacemaker; located in right atrial wall near SVC entry. Intrinsic rate **≈ 100/min** (parasympathetic vagal tone lowers it to ~70 bpm).
2. **Atrial muscle** — Conducts depolarisation wave; causes **atrial systole**.
3. **Atrio-Ventricular (AV) Node** — Introduces a **~100 ms delay** allowing ventricular filling.
4. **Bundle of His** — Penetrates the fibrous skeleton; only electrical bridge between atria and ventricles.
5. **Right & Left Bundle Branches** — Run along interventricular septum.
6. **Purkinje Fibres** — Spread through ventricular myocardium; fastest conduction (≈ 2–4 m/s) — guarantees near-synchronous ventricular contraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the AV-node delay matters (engineering view):** It is a *temporal de-multiplexer* that sequences atrial and ventricular contraction; without it, simultaneous contraction would halve the ejection efficiency.

### 2.1.2 Cardiac Cycle Phases

| Phase | Mechanical Event | Valve State | Duration (HR=75) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atrial systole | Atria contract | AV open, SL closed | ≈ 0.1 s |
| Isovolumetric contraction | Ventricles pressurise, no volume change | All closed | ≈ 0.05 s |
| Ventricular ejection | Blood ejected | AV closed, SL open | ≈ 0.3 s |
| Isovolumetric relaxation | Ventricles relax, no volume change | All closed | ≈ 0.05 s |
| Passive filling (diastasis) | Blood flows down pressure gradient | AV open, SL closed | ≈ 0.3 s |
| **Total Cycle Time** | | | **≈ 0.8 s** |

### 2.1.3 Cardiac Output — The Master Equation

$$CO = HR \times SV$$

Where **SV** (Stroke Volume) depends on three determinants (Frank–Starling mechanism):

$$SV = EDV - ESV$$

---

## 2.2 The Lungs — Mechanics of Breathing

### 2.2.1 Pressure Relationships

The lungs behave as a *passive elastic structure* expanded by the respiratory muscles. The pressure balance is:

$$P_{alv} = P_{ip} + \frac{2T}{r} \quad \text{(Laplace, simplified)}$$

For inspiration:
- Diaphragm contracts → thoracic volume ↑ → **intrapleural pressure** drops from ≈ −5 cm H₂O to ≈ −8 cm H₂O.
- **Alveolar pressure** drops below atmospheric (~ −1 cm H₂O) → air flows in.

For expiration (passive):
- Diaphragm relaxes → thoracic volume ↓ → **P_alv** rises above atmospheric (~ +1 cm H₂O) → air flows out.

### 2.2.2 Lung Compliance (C)

$$C = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta P} \quad \text{[L/cm H}_2\text{O]}$$

Normal compliance ≈ **0.2 L/cm H₂O**. Emphysema ↑ C (floppy); fibrosis ↓ C (stiff).

### 2.2.3 Key Volumes (already tabulated in §1.3)

**Vital Capacity (VC):**
$$VC = TV + IRV + ERV$$

**Total Lung Capacity (TLC):**
$$TLC = TV + IRV + ERV + RV$$

---

## 2.3 The Muscles — Sliding-Filament Theory

### 2.3.1 Excitation-Contraction Coupling (Skeletal)

1. **AP arrives** at neuromuscular junction → ACh released.
2. **ACh binds** nicotinic receptors at motor end-plate → End-plate potential (local depolarisation).
3. **AP propagates** along sarcolemma → down T-tubules.
4. **DHP receptors** mechanically coupled to **ryanodine receptors** on sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) → SR releases **Ca²⁺**.
5. **Ca²⁺ binds troponin-C** → tropomyosin shifts → myosin heads can bind actin.
6. **Cross-bridge cycling**: Power stroke → ADP + Pᵢ released → ATP re-binds → detachment.
7. **Ca²⁺ reuptake** into SR by SERCA pump → relaxation.

### 2.3.2 Force-Length Relationship

Maximum tetanic force is generated when sarcomere length ≈ **2.0–2.2 µm** (optimal actin-myosin overlap).

---

## 2.4 Sources of Bioelectric Potential — Theory

### 2.4.1 The Nernst Equation (Single-Ion Equilibrium)

For a single ionic species `X` with charge `z`, the **Nernst potential** E_x is the membrane voltage at which there is *no net flux* of that ion:

$$E_x = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

Switching to log₁₀ and using the body-temperature constant (61.5/z mV at 37 °C):

$$E_x \;[\text{mV}] = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

**Standard values (giant squid axon / typical neuron):**

| Ion | [out] (mM) | [in] (mM) | E_x (mV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| K⁺ | 4 | 140 | **−94** |
| Na⁺ | 145 | 12 | **+60** |
| Cl⁻ | 120 | 4 | **−89** |
| Ca²⁺ | 1.2 | 10⁻⁴ | **+129** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reality check:** The cell’s measured resting potential (≈ −70 mV) lies *between* E_K and E_Na — proof that more than one ion is permeant. The Nernst equation alone cannot explain this; we need the **Goldman equation**.

### 2.4.2 The Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation

When the membrane is permeable to **K⁺, Na⁺ and Cl⁻** with relative permeabilities P_K, P_Na, P_Cl:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln \frac{P_K [K^+]_{out} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K [K^+]_{in} + P_{Na} [Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl} [Cl^-]_{out}}$$

This is the *single most important equation* in cellular electrophysiology and is asked repeatedly in KTU exams.

### 2.4.3 Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)

The RMP arises because at rest, the membrane is **≈ 25–100× more permeable to K⁺** than to Na⁺. Hence V_m is “dragged” toward E_K but never quite reaches it. The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump (3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in per cycle) maintains the ionic gradients by **active transport**, expending **≈ 25 %** of the body’s total resting ATP.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition (water-tank analogy):** Imagine two connected water tanks — Tank K (potassium) and Tank Na (sodium). Tank K is huge and has a wide pipe (K⁺ leak channels) — most water flows there. Tank Na has a narrow pipe, but a *pump* continuously lifts water back uphill. The *combined water level* in the system corresponds to the resting voltage.

### 2.4.4 Action Potential (AP) — Phases

| Phase | Event | Dominant Ion | Voltage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0 — Depolarisation** | Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open rapidly | Na⁺ IN | −70 → +30 mV |
| **1 — Initial repolarisation** | Na⁺ inactivate; transient K⁺ out (I_to) | K⁺ OUT | +30 → 0 mV |
| **2 — Plateau (cardiac only)** | L-type Ca²⁺ channels open; delayed K⁺ efflux | Ca²⁺ IN / K⁺ OUT | ≈ 0 mV (sustained) |
| **3 — Rapid repolarisation** | Delayed-rectifier K⁺ channels dominate | K⁺ OUT | 0 → −80 mV |
| **4 — Resting / Hyperpolarisation** | K⁺ channels still open; Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase restores gradients | K⁺ leak | ≈ −70 to −90 mV |

**Nerve AP** lacks the plateau (Phases 1–3 collapse into one sharp spike of **1–2 ms**).
**Cardiac ventricular AP** is **200–300 ms** long due to the Ca²⁺ plateau — *this is what gives the heart its refractory “twitch” property* and prevents tetanic contraction.

### 2.4.5 Threshold & All-or-None Law

- **Threshold (V_th)** ≈ **−55 mV** in neurons.
- Once exceeded, the AP fires to its full amplitude regardless of stimulus strength — **all-or-none**.
- Strength is encoded in **frequency** of APs, not their amplitude.

### 2.4.6 Refractory Periods

- **Absolute Refractory Period (ARP):** No new AP possible (Na⁺ channels inactivated). Corresponds to phases 0–2.
- **Relative Refractory Period (RRP):** Stronger-than-normal stimulus can elicit AP (some Na⁺ channels recovered, K⁺ still open).

### 2.4.7 Propagation Velocity

For an unmyelinated axon:

$$v \propto \sqrt{\frac{d}{R_i \cdot C_m}}$$

For a myelinated axon, propagation is **saltatory** (jumps between Nodes of Ranvier) and reaches **≈ 100 m/s** in large motor fibres.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — Print This!** All the equations you need for a Module 1 question are here.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_x = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln \dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$ | Nernst (single ion) | V (or mV) |
| 2 | $E_x = \dfrac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\dfrac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$ | Nernst at 37 °C | mV |
| 3 | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | Goldman-H-K | V (or mV) |
| 4 | $CO = HR \times SV$ | Cardiac output | L/min |
| 5 | $SV = EDV - ESV$ | Stroke volume | mL |
| 6 | $VC = TV + IRV + ERV$ | Vital capacity | mL |
| 7 | $TLC = VC + RV$ | Total lung capacity | mL |
| 8 | $C = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta P}$ | Lung compliance | L/cmH₂O |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ | Energy stored in membrane capacitor | Joules |
| 10 | $v \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{R_i C_m}}$ | AP propagation velocity (unmyelinated) | m/s |
| 11 | $I_{ion} = g_{ion}(V_m - E_{ion})$ | Ionic current (Hodgkin–Huxley formalism) | A/cm² |
| 12 | $\tau = R_m C_m$ | Membrane time constant | s |
| 13 | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}$ | Length constant | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠ Markdown Safety Note:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` inside math expressions (e.g., for absolute value or "such that") has been replaced with $\vert$ or $\mid$ to keep the table clean. Do **not** use raw `|` inside table cells — it breaks Markdown table parsing.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why BME Students Must Know This

| Physiological Quantity | Clinical / Engineering Tool that Measures It |
|---|---|
| Cardiac electrical activity | **ECG / EKG** (12-lead) |
| Brain electrical activity | **EEG** (10–20 system) |
| Muscle electrical activity | **EMG** (surface / needle) |
| Eye electrical activity | **EOG / ERG** |
| Lung volumes & flow | **Spirometer, Peak-flow meter** |
| Gas exchange | **Pulse oximeter, ABG analyser** |
| Heart sounds & murmurs | **Phonocardiograph, stethoscope** |
| AP conduction in nerves | **Nerve conduction study (NCS)** |

> [!TIP]
> Every diagnostic instrument above is fundamentally a *transducer + signal conditioner + display*. Knowing the source of the bioelectric signal tells you what frequency, amplitude and noise to design for.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (Thermodynamic)

We start from the **electrochemical potential** of an ion X with charge z, which must be equal on both sides of the membrane at equilibrium (Δμ = 0).

**Step 1 — Write the chemical potential difference (concentration gradient):**
$$\Delta\mu_{chem} = RT \ln \frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}}$$

**Step 2 — Write the electrical potential energy difference:**
$$\Delta\mu_{elec} = zFV_m \quad \text{where } V_m = V_{in} - V_{out}$$

**Step 3 — At equilibrium, total electrochemical potential difference is zero:**
$$\Delta\mu_{chem} + \Delta\mu_{elec} = 0$$

Substituting:
$$RT \ln \frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}} + zF V_m = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for V_m:**
$$V_m = -\frac{RT}{zF}\ln \frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}} = \frac{RT}{zF}\ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert natural log to log₁₀ using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:**
$$V_m = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the constant at T = 310 K (37 °C body temp):**
$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$

Hence the practical form:
$$\boxed{\,E_x \;[\text{mV}] = \frac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic explained:** Step 1 is purely chemical (entropy-driven); Step 2 is purely electrical (work to move charge). At equilibrium the two must cancel — this is the *Nernst condition*. Steps 5–6 are unit/scale conversions.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Nernst Potential for K⁺

Given: [K⁺]_out = 4 mM, [K⁺]_in = 140 mM, z = +1.

**Step 1 — Plug into Nernst:**
$$E_K = \frac{61.5}{+1}\log_{10}\frac{4}{140}$$

**Step 2 — Compute ratio:**
$$\frac{4}{140} = 0.02857$$

**Step 3 — Take log₁₀:**
$$\log_{10}(0.02857) = -1.5441$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 61.5:**
$$E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.5441) = -94.96 \text{ mV}$$

**Answer:** **E_K ≈ −95 mV** ✅ (matches standard textbook value −94 mV).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The GHK equation is *not* derived from scratch in a single lecture. It emerges from integrating the **constant-field assumption** of Goldman (1943) over the membrane. The full derivation uses the Nernst-Planck flux equation and is beyond KTU scope, but we can show the *result* and its logical structure so it doesn’t feel like a black box.

**Step 1 — Start from Nernst–Planck for 1-D flux of ion i:**
$$J_i = -D_i \left( \frac{dC_i}{dx} + \frac{z_i F}{RT} C_i \frac{dV}{dx} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the *constant-field* assumption: $dV/dx = V_m / a$** (where a = membrane thickness) and integrate from x = 0 (inside) to x = a (outside).

**Step 3 — Solve the first-order linear ODE for C_i(x).** This is a routine but lengthy integration; the result is the **permeability form** of flux.

**Step 4 — Sum fluxes over all permeant ions (K, Na, Cl).** At steady state, the net current $I_{total} = 0$, so:
$$\sum_i z_i F J_i = 0$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic manipulation gives the GHK equation (note: I will skip steps 3 and 4 algebra, but show the result):**
$$V_m = \frac{RT}{F}\ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering View:** GHK is the *weighted average* of individual Nernst potentials, where the weights are the **permeabilities** of the membrane to each ion. The cell membrane behaves like a *resistive voltage divider* where the resistor values are $1/P_i$.

### 3.3.1 Worked GHK Example (Typical Squid Axon)

Given:
- [K⁺]_o = 20, [K⁺]_i = 400, P_K = 1.0
- [Na⁺]_o = 440, [Na⁺]_i = 50, P_Na = 0.04
- [Cl⁻]_o = 560, [Cl⁻]_i = 50, P_Cl = 0.45
- T = 293 K (room temp squid exp.)

**Step 1 — Numerator (outside):**
$$N = (1.0)(20) + (0.04)(440) + (0.45)(50)$$
$$N = 20 + 17.6 + 22.5 = 60.1$$

**Step 2 — Denominator (inside):**
$$D = (1.0)(400) + (0.04)(50) + (0.45)(560)$$
$$D = 400 + 2.0 + 252.0 = 654.0$$

**Step 3 — Compute RT/F at 293 K:**
$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 293}{96485} = 0.02525 \text{ V} = 25.25 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ratio:**
$$\frac{N}{D} = \frac{60.1}{654.0} = 0.09189$$

**Step 5 — Take natural log:**
$$\ln(0.09189) = -2.388$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by 25.25 mV:**
$$V_m = 25.25 \times (-2.388) = -60.30 \text{ mV}$$

**Result:** $V_m \approx -60 \text{ mV}$ (the measured resting potential for the squid giant axon is ≈ −60 to −70 mV, validating the model). ✅

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Action Potentials in Different Tissues

| Parameter | Skeletal Muscle | Cardiac (Ventricular) | Smooth Muscle | Neuron |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resting V_m | ≈ −90 mV | ≈ −90 mV | ≈ −50 mV | ≈ −70 mV |
| Peak V_m | ≈ +30 mV | ≈ +30 mV | ≈ 0 mV | ≈ +30 mV |
| AP duration | 1–2 ms | **200–300 ms** | Variable | 1–2 ms |
| Plateau (Phase 2) | **Absent** | **Present (Ca²⁺)** | Variable | **Absent** |
| Tetanus possible? | **Yes** | **No (long ARP)** | Yes (slow) | N/A |
| Pacemaker activity | No | **Yes (SA node)** | Some | No |
| Threshold | ≈ −55 mV | ≈ −70 mV (SA) | ≈ −40 mV | ≈ −55 mV |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the cardiac plateau is engineering-critical:** It *prevents* premature re-excitation (long absolute refractory period) — guaranteeing the ventricle completes its contraction-relaxation cycle before another electrical wave can pass. Without it, the heart would fibrillate.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Practice — Cardiac Output

**Problem:** A patient has HR = 72 bpm and end-diastolic volume EDV = 120 mL, end-systolic volume ESV = 50 mL. Calculate cardiac output.

**Step 1 — Compute SV:**
$$SV = EDV - ESV = 120 - 50 = 70 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 2 — Compute CO:**
$$CO = HR \times SV = 72 \times 70 = 5040 \text{ mL/min} \approx 5.04 \text{ L/min}$$

**Interpretation:** This lies in the **normal range of 4–8 L/min** for a resting adult.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Practice — Vital Capacity

**Problem:** Spirometry gives TV = 0.5 L, IRV = 3.0 L, ERV = 1.1 L, RV = 1.2 L. Find VC and TLC.

**Step 1 — VC:**
$$VC = 0.5 + 3.0 + 1.1 = 4.6 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2 — TLC:**
$$TLC = 4.6 + 1.2 = 5.8 \text{ L}$$

**Verification:** Both values fall within the **normal adult ranges** (VC ≈ 4.6 L; TLC ≈ 5.8 L). ✅
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid** syntax. Node IDs are alphanumeric (per the safety rules) and labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text (no embedded Markdown bold/italics/HTML tables).

## 4.1 Heart Conduction System (Signal Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SA[SINOATRIAL NODE - PACEMAKER 100 BPM] --> AT[ATRIAL MYOCARDIUM]
    AT --> AV[AV NODE - DELAY 100 MS]
    AV --> BH[BUNDLE OF HIS]
    BH --> RB[RIGHT BUNDLE BRANCH]
    BH --> LB[LEFT BUNDLE BRANCH]
    RB --> PF1[PURKINJE FIBERS - RV]
    LB --> PF2[PURKINJE FIBERS - LV]
    PF1 --> V1[VENTRICULAR CONTRACTION]
    PF2 --> V2[VENTRICULAR CONTRACTION]
    style SA fill:#ff9999
    style AV fill:#ffcc99
    style BH fill:#ffff99
    style PF1 fill:#99ff99
    style PF2 fill:#99ff99
    style V1 fill:#99ccff
    style V2 fill:#99ccff
```

**Reading the diagram:** Electrical impulse begins at the SA node → spreads across both atria → is delayed at the AV node (allowing atrial contraction to fill the ventricles) → passes through the Bundle of His → splits into right and left bundle branches → reaches the Purkinje fibres → triggers near-synchronous ventricular contraction.

---

## 4.2 Action Potential — Phase Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[RESTING PHASE 4 - Vm MINUS 70 MV] -->|Stimulus past threshold| D[DEPOLARIZATION PHASE 0 - Na CHANNELS OPEN - Vm RISES TO PLUS 30 MV]
    D --> I1[PHASE 1 INITIAL REPOLARIZATION - Na INACTIVATE - I_to K EFFLUX]
    I1 --> P[PHASE 2 PLATEAU CARDIAC ONLY - Ca INFLUX BALANCES K EFFLUX]
    I1 --> R2[PHASE 3 RAPID REPOLARIZATION NERVE - K EFFLUX]
    P --> R3[PHASE 3 RAPID REPOLARIZATION CARDIAC - K EFFLUX]
    R2 --> HP[HYPERPOLARIZATION - Vm BRIEFLY BELOW RESTING]
    R3 --> RP[RECOVERY - NaK PUMP RESTORES GRADIENTS]
    HP --> R
    RP --> R
    style R fill:#cce5ff
    style D fill:#ff9999
    style P fill:#ffcc99
    style R3 fill:#99ff99
    style RP fill:#e6ccff
```

**Reading the diagram:** Phase 0 = upstroke, Phase 1 = notch, Phase 2 = plateau (cardiac only), Phase 3 = downstroke, Phase 4 = return to rest. The hysteresis loop illustrates the cell’s excursion around the resting state.

---

## 4.3 Lung Volumes and Capacities (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    TLC[TOTAL LUNG CAPACITY 5800 ML] --> VC[VITAL CAPACITY 4600 ML]
    TLC --> RV[RESIDUAL VOLUME 1200 ML]
    VC --> TV[TIDAL VOLUME 500 ML]
    VC --> IRV[INSPIRATORY RESERVE 3000 ML]
    VC --> ERV[EXPIRATORY RESERVE 1100 ML]
    TV --> B1[NORMAL INSPIRATION]
    TV --> B2[NORMAL EXPIRATION]
    IRV --> B3[FORCED INSPIRATION]
    ERV --> B4[FORCED EXPIRATION]
    style TLC fill:#ff6666
    style VC fill:#ffcc66
    style TV fill:#99ff99
    style IRV fill:#99ccff
    style ERV fill:#cc99ff
    style RV fill:#cccccc
```

**Reading the diagram:** The hierarchy shows that VC is a *component* of TLC, while TV, IRV and ERV are *components* of VC. RV is the irreducible air remaining after forced expiration.

---

## 4.4 Ionic Basis of Resting and Action Potential (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    EXT[EXTRACELLULAR SPACE] -->|Na 145, K 4, Cl 120 mM| MEM[PLASMA MEMBRANE LIPID BILAYER]
    INT[INTRACELLULAR SPACE] -->|Na 12, K 140, Cl 4 mM| MEM
    MEM --> KLEAK[K LEAK CHANNELS - DOMINANT AT REST]
    MEM --> NAG[V Na CHANNELS - CLOSED AT REST]
    MEM --> NAPUMP[NaK ATPASE PUMP 3 Na OUT 2 K IN]
    KLEAK -->|K efflux pushes Vm toward E_K| VM1[RESTING Vm APPROX MINUS 70 MV]
    NAPUMP -->|Maintains gradients actively| VM1
    NAG -->|Open on stimulus| VAP[ACTION POTENTIAL Na INFLUX PEAK PLUS 30 MV]
    VAP -->|K channels repolarise| VM2[REPOLARIZATION BACK TO REST]
    VM2 --> NAPUMP
    style VM1 fill:#cce5ff
    style VAP fill:#ff9999
    style VM2 fill:#99ff99
    style NAPUMP fill:#ffcc99
```

**Reading the diagram:** At rest, K⁺ leak dominates → V_m is close to E_K (negative). The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase continuously pumps ions against their gradients. When a stimulus opens voltage-gated Na⁺ channels, Na⁺ rushes in → V_m shoots up (action potential). K⁺ channels then repolarise the cell, and the pump restores ionic distribution.

---

## 4.5 Skeletal Muscle — Excitation-Contraction Coupling

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[NERVE AP ARRIVES AT NMJ] --> B[ACH RELEASED INTO SYNAPTIC CLEFT]
    B --> C[ACh BINDS NICOTINIC RECEPTOR]
    C --> D[ENDPLATE POTENTIAL LOCAL DEPOLARIZATION]
    D --> E[AP TRAVELS DOWN SARCOLEMMA INTO T TUBULES]
    E --> F[DHP RECEPTOR ACTIVATED MECHANICALLY]
    F --> G[RYANODINE RECEPTOR OPENS]
    G --> H[CA RELEASED FROM SARCOPLASMIC RETICULUM]
    H --> I[CA BINDS TROPONIN C]
    I --> J[TROPOMYOSIN SHIFTS EXPOSING ACTIN SITES]
    J --> K[MYOSIN CROSS BRIDGE CYCLING - ATP HYDROLYSIS]
    K --> L[MUSCLE SHORTENS FORCE GENERATED]
    L --> M[CA REUPTAKE BY SERCA PUMP]
    M --> N[MUSCLE RELAXATION]
    style A fill:#cce5ff
    style H fill:#ff9999
    style K fill:#99ff99
    style N fill:#ffcc99
```

**Reading the diagram:** The chain from neural input to mechanical output — note that **Ca²⁺ is the central messenger** linking electrical excitation to mechanical contraction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Reminder:** Part A = 3-mark short-answer (≈ 10-word to 4-line answer). Part B = 14-mark long-answer with internal choice between two full questions, each split into 7+7 sub-parts as per KTU ESE pattern.

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (with Model Answers)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define resting membrane potential. Why is its value closer to the Nernst potential of K⁺ than of Na⁺?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Resting membrane potential (RMP) is the steady-state voltage difference across the plasma membrane of a living cell when it is not generating an action potential — typically **−70 mV** in a neuron. It is closer to **E_K (≈ −95 mV)** than to **E_Na (≈ +60 mV)** because at rest, the membrane is **≈ 25–100 times more permeable to K⁺** than to Na⁺ (due to the dominance of K⁺ leak channels). According to the Goldman equation, the more permeable ion dominates the weighted average — so V_m is “dragged” toward E_K. The Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump actively maintains the ion gradients, but does not determine the value of V_m.

*(Marks: Definition 1 m, K-permeability reasoning 1 m, Pump role 1 m.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**List the components of the cardiac conduction system in the order in which the impulse travels. Mention the intrinsic rate of the SA node.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The cardiac impulse travels in the order: **Sino-Atrial (SA) node → Atrial muscle → Atrio-Ventricular (AV) node → Bundle of His → Right and Left Bundle Branches → Purkinje fibres → Ventricular myocardium.** The intrinsic (automatic) rate of the SA node is **≈ 100 beats per minute**, but vagal tone reduces the effective resting heart rate to **60–100 bpm**.

*(Marks: Six correct components 2 m, Intrinsic SA rate 1 m.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** Explain the origin of the resting membrane potential in a living cell. Derive the **Nernst equation** and calculate the Nernst potential for **K⁺** at 37 °C given [K⁺]_out = 4 mM and [K⁺]_in = 140 mM. Why does the measured RMP differ from this value, and how is this explained by the **Goldman equation**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **phases of the cardiac ventricular action potential** and contrast it with a typical **neuronal action potential**. Explain why the cardiac AP has a long plateau phase and discuss its physiological significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for A(a) — 7 marks**

**Origin of RMP (2 marks):**
- All living cells maintain an **asymmetric distribution of ions** across the plasma membrane.
- At rest: [K⁺]_in ≫ [K⁺]_out; [Na⁺]_out ≫ [Na⁺]_in; [Cl⁻]_out ≫ [Cl⁻]_in; large anionic proteins trapped inside.
- Two forces act on ions: (i) **chemical concentration gradient** (Fick), (ii) **electrical gradient** (Coulomb).
- At rest, K⁺ diffuses *outward* down its gradient, leaving behind negative charges → **negative interior**.
- Equilibrium is reached when electrical force balances chemical force — the resulting voltage is the **K⁺ Nernst potential E_K**.

**Derivation of Nernst equation (2 marks):**
At electrochemical equilibrium for ion X with charge z:
$$\Delta\mu = RT \ln \frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}} + zF V_m = 0$$
Solving for V_m:
$$V_m = \frac{RT}{zF}\ln \frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} = \frac{61.5}{z}\log_{10}\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}} \text{ mV at 37 °C}$$

**Calculation (2 marks):**
For K⁺ (z = +1):
$$E_K = 61.5 \times \log_{10}\frac{4}{140} = 61.5 \times (-1.544) = -94.96 \text{ mV} \approx -95 \text{ mV}$$

**Discrepancy with measured RMP & Goldman explanation (1 mark):**
Measured RMP ≈ −70 mV (not −95 mV) because membrane is *not* exclusively K⁺-permeable. The **Goldman-H-K equation** gives V_m as a permeability-weighted average of the Nernst potentials of all permeant ions. With P_K ≫ P_Na, the result lies between E_K and E_Na, closer to E_K — explaining the observed −70 mV.

---

#### **Model Solution for A(b) — 7 marks**

**Cardiac ventricular AP phases (3 marks):**

| Phase | Event | Dominant Ion | Voltage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Rapid depolarisation | Na⁺ IN | −90 → +30 mV |
| 1 | Initial repolarisation | Na⁺ inactivate, I_to K⁺ OUT | +30 → 0 mV |
| 2 | **Plateau** | L-type Ca²⁺ IN = K⁺ OUT | ≈ 0 mV (sustained 200–300 ms) |
| 3 | Rapid repolarisation | Delayed-rectifier K⁺ OUT | 0 → −90 mV |
| 4 | Resting | K⁺ leak + NaK pump | −90 mV |

**Neuronal AP contrast (2 marks):**
Neuronal AP is a brief **1–2 ms spike** with phases 0, 3, 4 only (no plateau). The membrane returns to rest rapidly because there is no sustained Ca²⁺ influx.

**Plateau significance (2 marks):**
- The L-type **Ca²⁺** influx during phase 2 is the trigger for **Ca²⁺-induced Ca²⁺ release** from the SR, providing the bulk of activator Ca²⁺ for **myocardial contraction**.
- The long plateau → long **absolute refractory period** → prevents re-excitation during contraction → **prevents tetanus**, allowing proper diastolic filling.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner’s Pitfall (Module 1 — Cardiac AP):**
> Students frequently confuse the roles of Na⁺ and Ca²⁺ in cardiac vs neuronal AP. **Mnemonic:** *“Nerve fires on Na⁺; Heart contracts on Ca²⁺.”* Losing the distinction between phase 0 (Na⁺) and phase 2 (Ca²⁺) typically costs **2–3 marks** in the 7-mark sub-question. State the ions explicitly in a table — examiners love tables.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Describe the **anatomy and conduction pathway of the human heart**. With a block diagram, explain the **cardiac cycle** in terms of pressure–volume loops, systole and diastole, and the role of valves. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List the **lung volumes and capacities** with their typical values. Derive an expression for **lung compliance** and explain the pressure–volume behaviour of the respiratory system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B(a) — 7 marks**

**Anatomy of heart (2 marks):**
- Four-chambered: 2 atria (receiving) + 2 ventricles (pumping).
- Right side → pulmonary circulation; Left side → systemic circulation.
- Walls: endocardium, myocardium (thickest in LV), pericardium.

**Conduction pathway (2 marks):**
SA node → atrial muscle → AV node (delay) → Bundle of His → R/L bundle branches → Purkinje fibres → ventricular myocardium.

**Cardiac cycle and P–V loop (3 marks):**
- **Atrial systole** (≈ 0.1 s): Atria contract; ventricles fill from EDV.
- **Isovolumetric contraction** (≈ 0.05 s): Ventricles pressurise with all valves closed; volume constant.
- **Ventricular ejection** (≈ 0.3 s): When ventricular P > aortic P, semilunar valves open; blood ejected until ESV.
- **Isovolumetric relaxation** (≈ 0.05 s): All valves closed; pressure falls.
- **Passive filling / diastasis** (≈ 0.3 s): AV valves open; blood flows down gradient.

**P–V loop:** ABCD cycle — A (mitral closure) → B (aortic valve opens) → C (aortic valve closes) → D (mitral valve opens). The enclosed *area = external work done by ventricle* per beat.

---

#### **Model Solution for B(b) — 7 marks**

**Lung volumes and capacities (3 marks):**
(TV = 0.5 L, IRV = 3.0 L, ERV = 1.1 L, RV = 1.2 L; VC = TV+IRV+ERV ≈ 4.6 L; TLC = VC+RV ≈ 5.8 L — refer to §1.3 table.)

**Compliance derivation (2 marks):**
Compliance is defined as the ratio of volume change to the pressure change required to produce it:
$$C = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta P}$$
- Units: L per cm H₂O (or L/kPa in SI).
- Normal lung compliance ≈ **0.2 L/cm H₂O** (steep, easily inflated).
- Emphysema → ↑ C (loss of elastic recoil, “floppy” lung).
- Pulmonary fibrosis → ↓ C (stiff lung, needs greater pressure change).

**Pressure–volume behaviour (2 marks):**
- The P–V curve of the lung is **S-shaped (sigmoidal)**.
- In the **mid-volume range** (FRC region), the curve is steepest → highest compliance → most efficient tidal breathing.
- At extremes (near RV and TLC), compliance drops sharply (Laplace + collagen/elastin limits).
- The slope at any point = local compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner’s Pitfall (Lung Volumes):**
> A *very common* mistake is to include RV in vital capacity. **RV is NOT part of VC** because it cannot be exhaled. The correct expression is $VC = TV + IRV + ERV$ and $TLC = VC + RV$. Confusing these two formulae can cost **2 marks** easily.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s General Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks in Module 1 (Biomedical Engineering, OEAET612):**
> 1. **Using the wrong Nernst constant** — students write 26.7 or 59.2 instead of 61.5 mV (the 61.5 is at 37 °C, body temperature; 59.2 is at 25 °C).
> 2. **Mixing up E_K and V_m** — E_K is the *equilibrium* potential (−95 mV) for K⁺ alone; V_m is the *actual* resting voltage (−70 mV) of the live cell.
> 3. **Writing the Goldman equation upside down** — the ratio is *P[ion]_out : P[ion]_in*, not inverted. Always check the sign of V_m at the end.
> 4. **Omitting the role of Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** — passive permeability alone cannot sustain RMP; the pump must be mentioned to earn full credit.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams in long-answer questions** — a 14-mark cardiac conduction question without a labelled block diagram typically loses 2–3 marks; KTU examiners award marks for **neat labelled diagrams explicitly**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print This & Memorise Before the Exam**

**Heart (must-know):**
- ☐ Four chambers, two pumps (right = pulmonary, left = systemic).
- ☐ Conduction: **SA → Atria → AV (delay) → Bundle of His → Bundle branches → Purkinje → Ventricles**.
- ☐ SA node intrinsic rate ≈ **100 bpm**; resting rate **60–100 bpm**.
- ☐ **CO = HR × SV**; typical adult CO ≈ **5 L/min**.
- ☐ AV node delay ≈ **100 ms** — essential for sequential contraction.

**Lungs (must-know):**
- ☐ Primary muscle of inspiration = **diaphragm**.
- ☐ Standard adult volumes: TV=500 mL, IRV=3000 mL, ERV=1100 mL, RV=1200 mL.
- ☐ **VC = TV + IRV + ERV ≈ 4.6 L** (RV is NOT in VC).
- ☐ **TLC = VC + RV ≈ 5.8 L**.
- ☐ Compliance $C = \Delta V / \Delta P$; normal ≈ 0.2 L/cm H₂O.

**Muscles (must-know):**
- ☐ Three types: **skeletal, cardiac, smooth** (striated vs non-striated; voluntary vs involuntary).
- ☐ **Ca²⁺** is the key messenger of excitation–contraction coupling.
- ☐ ATP required for: cross-bridge cycling (myosin ATPase) and Ca²⁺ reuptake (SERCA).
- ☐ Optimal sarcomere length for max force ≈ **2.0–2.2 µm**.

**Bioelectric Potentials (must-know — highest weightage):**
- ☐ RMP typical values: neuron ≈ **−70 mV**, ventricular myocyte ≈ **−90 mV**, smooth muscle ≈ **−50 mV**.
- ☐ Nernst: $E_x = (61.5/z) \log_{10}([X]_{out}/[X]_{in})$ at 37 °C.
- ☐ Goldman: $V_m = (RT/F) \ln \frac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$.
- ☐ At rest, P_K ≫ P_Na → RMP near E_K.
- ☐ **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase**: 3 Na⁺ out + 2 K⁺ in per ATP → electrogenic → consumes **≈ 25 % of resting body ATP**.
- ☐ AP phases (0–4) and ions: **0 = Na⁺ in**, **2 = Ca²⁺ in (cardiac only)**, **3 = K⁺ out**, **4 = K⁺ leak + pump**.
- ☐ Neuronal AP duration **1–2 ms**; cardiac ventricular AP **200–300 ms**.
- ☐ Threshold ≈ **−55 mV**; all-or-none law; strength encoded in **frequency**.
- ☐ **Absolute refractory period** prevents retrograde AP and tetanus (especially in cardiac muscle).
- ☐ Propagation: continuous in unmyelinated axons; **saltatory** in myelinated (jumps at Nodes of Ranvier).

**KTU Exam Strategy Tips:**
- ☐ Always draw a **neat labelled block diagram** in 7+7 mark questions — examiners award 2–3 marks purely for illustration.
- ☐ Memorise the **Nernst and Goldman equations verbatim** — they appear in nearly every exam.
- ☐ For numericals, always **state the constants** (R, T, F) and show the **arithmetic step-by-step**.
- ☐ In action potential questions, always **tabulate phases, ions and voltages** — clean tables earn full credit.
- ☐ End with a **physiological/clinical significance statement** (e.g., “The plateau phase ensures the heart cannot be tetanised”) to grab the final 1–2 marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Mastery Statement:** *A student who can (i) draw the heart conduction system, (ii) write the Nernst and Goldman equations from memory, (iii) tabulate the five phases of the cardiac action potential with ions, and (iv) compute a Nernst potential numerically — has already secured 80 % of the marks available on this module.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Propagation of Action Potentials. Bioelectric Potentials (Introduction Only) - ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET612 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Propagation of Action Potentials. Bioelectric Potentials (Introduction Only) - ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Propagation of Action Potentials — Definition

An **action potential (AP)** is a rapid, transient, all-or-nothing depolarization wave that travels along the membrane of an excitable cell (nerve or muscle fiber). The **propagation of an action potential** is the process by which the local depolarization at one point on the membrane is regenerated at the adjacent point, creating a self-propagating wave of electrical activity that travels away from the site of initiation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, propagation refers to the **electrochemical wave-front movement** governed by the local-circuit currents established between the active (depolarized) region and the adjacent resting regions of the cell membrane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Propagation deals with **two distinct mechanisms**:
> 1. **Continuous (Electrotonic) Conduction** — occurs in *unmyelinated* axons.
> 2. **Saltatory Conduction** — occurs in *myelinated* axons (jumping between Nodes of Ranvier).

## 1.2 Bioelectric Potentials — Introduction

**Bioelectric potentials** are the electrical potentials (voltages) generated across the membranes of living cells due to the differential distribution of ions (primarily Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Cl⁻) maintained by ion channels and active transport pumps (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase). The two principal bioelectric potentials of clinical and engineering importance are:

- **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP):** Typically **−70 mV** (inside negative w.r.t. outside) in nerve cells.
- **Action Potential (AP):** A brief reversal of polarity reaching about **+30 to +40 mV**, lasting **1–2 ms** in neurons.

> [!NOTE]
> The RMP can be quantitatively derived using the **Nernst Equation** for a single ion, and the **Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Equation** for multiple ions acting simultaneously.

## 1.3 Electrocardiogram (ECG / EKG) — Definition

The **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is a non-invasive graphical recording of the **summed electrical activity of the heart**, obtained from surface electrodes placed on the body. It represents the time-varying potential differences produced by the **systematic depolarization and repolarization** of the cardiac muscle (myocardium) during each cardiac cycle.

**Father of ECG:** **Willem Einthoven** (Nobel Prize, 1924).

> [!NOTE]
> Standard clinical ECG paper speed: **25 mm/s**; vertical calibration: **10 mm = 1 mV**.
> Therefore: 1 small box = 1 mm = **0.04 s (40 ms)**; 1 large box = 5 mm = **0.20 s (200 ms)**.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogies (First-Read Friendly)

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Quick Takeaway |
|---|---|---|
| **Action Potential Propagation** | Lighting the fuse of a firecracker — once the spark is set, the flame travels down the fuse on its own, igniting the next segment automatically. | The wave is **self-regenerating**; it does not weaken with distance. |
| **Resting Potential** | A loaded spring under tension, ready to snap. | The cell stores electrochemical energy via ion gradients. |
| **Saltatory Conduction** | Skipping stones on a pond — you do not push the water continuously, only at the points where the stone lands (Nodes of Ranvier). | Myelin **insulates** the axon; AP regenerates only at gaps → faster, less energy. |
| **ECG Waveform** | A slow-motion video of a stadium wave — every spectator stands up in a coordinated sequence; from above, you can record the wave passing around the stadium. | Each ECG wave corresponds to a **specific mass of cardiac tissue** depolarizing or repolarizing. |
| **Einthoven's Triangle** | A voltage divider network on the chest — both arms and the left leg act as the three corners of a triangle. | Allows vectorial analysis of cardiac electrical axis in the frontal plane. |

---

## 1.5 Standard Reference Values (Memorize for KTU)

- **Resting Membrane Potential:** **−70 mV** (nerve), **−90 mV** (cardiac ventricular).
- **Threshold potential:** **−55 mV** (typical neuron).
- **Nernst Potential (E_K):** **−94 mV** ; (E_Na): **+60 mV** at 37 °C.
- **Nerve conduction velocity:** **1–120 m/s** (depends on diameter and myelination).
- **Heart rate range:** **60–100 bpm**.
- **Normal PR interval:** **0.12–0.20 s**; **QRS duration:** **0.06–0.10 s**; **QT interval:** **0.36–0.44 s**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Biophysical Basis of Action Potential Propagation

Every action potential begins at a trigger zone (axon hillock for neurons, SA node for cardiac pacemaker cells). Once threshold is crossed, **voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** open, Na⁺ rushes in, and the local membrane voltage inverts. The local potential difference between the **active (depolarized) region** and the **adjacent resting region** now drives a **local circuit current** through the surrounding extracellular and intracellular fluid.

This local current passively depolarizes the neighboring membrane to threshold → its Na⁺ channels open → a new action potential is triggered there. The cycle repeats, and the wave of depolarization **propagates without decrement**.

> [!NOTE]
> The action potential is **regenerated, not just conducted**. Local-circuit currents are electrotonic (passive), but the AP itself is actively restored at every excitable point.

## 2.2 Types of Propagation

### 2.2.1 Continuous Conduction (Unmyelinated Axons)
- The AP is regenerated at **every successive patch** of membrane.
- Conduction is **slow** because every millimeter of membrane must fire.
- Typical velocity: **1–2 m/s** (small diameter C-fibers).

### 2.2.2 Saltatory Conduction (Myelinated Axons)
- **Myelin sheath** (formed by Schwann cells in PNS, oligodendrocytes in CNS) acts as a high-resistance, low-capacitance insulator.
- **Nodes of Ranvier** (≈ 1 µm gaps) are the **only** regions with high Na⁺ channel density.
- The AP effectively **jumps** from node to node → "saltare" (Latin: *to leap*).
- Conduction velocity: up to **120 m/s** (large α-motor neurons).

**Advantages of Saltatory Conduction:**
1. **Speed** — fewer membrane segments need to depolarize.
2. **Energy efficiency** — less Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase activity needed for ion restoration.
3. **Spatial fidelity** — signal preserved over long distances (e.g., 1 m sciatic nerve).

## 2.3 Quantitative Analysis — The Cable Equation

A nerve fiber behaves like an electrical **transmission line** with:
- **Specific membrane resistance** $r_m$ (Ω·cm).
- **Specific membrane capacitance** $c_m$ (µF/cm²).
- **Axial (intracellular) resistance** $r_i$ (Ω/cm).

Two key cable parameters govern propagation:

**Length constant (λ):** The distance over which a steady-state electrotonic potential decays to $\frac{1}{e} \approx 36.8\%$ of its value.

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}} \;\; \text{(cm)}$$

**Time constant (τ):** The time required for the membrane potential to reach $\frac{1}{e}$ of its final value when current is injected.

$$\tau = r_m \cdot c_m \;\; \text{(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher λ** → signal spreads further; **higher τ** → slower response. For a long, fast axon: large λ (good insulation) and small τ (fast membrane).

## 2.4 Conduction Velocity — Key Relations

For **unmyelinated fibers** (Hodgkin, 1954):
$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{2 R_i C_m}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{C_m}}$$
i.e., $v \propto \sqrt{d}$ (velocity increases as square root of diameter).

For **myelinated fibers** (Huxley, 1964 — Rushton's relation):
$$v \propto d \quad (\text{linear with diameter})$$
and **internodal distance ≈ 100 × axon diameter** is optimal for maximum velocity.

## 2.5 Factors Affecting Propagation Velocity

| Factor | Effect | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Axon diameter | ↑ velocity | Lower internal resistance |
| ↑ Myelination | ↑ velocity (large) | Reduced capacitance, increased $r_m$ |
| ↑ Temperature | ↑ velocity (within physiological range) | Faster ion-channel kinetics ($Q_{10} \approx 1.5$) |
| ↑ Membrane capacitance | ↓ velocity | More charge needed to depolarize |
| ↓ Membrane resistance | ↓ length constant | Signal decays faster |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — At a Glance

| Symbol | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_{ion}$ | $E = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_o}{[\text{ion}]_i}$ | **Nernst equation** — equilibrium potential | V (mV) |
| $E_{ion}$ (at 37 °C) | $E = \dfrac{61}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_o}{[\text{ion}]_i}$ | Nernst in **log base 10**, mV | mV |
| $V_m$ (GHK) | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F} \ln \dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | **Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz** resting potential | mV |
| $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_i}$ | **Length constant** | cm |
| $\tau$ | $\tau = r_m \, c_m$ | **Membrane time constant** | s |
| $v_{unmyel.}$ | $v \propto \sqrt{d}$ | Continuous conduction | m/s |
| $v_{myel.}$ | $v \propto d$ | Saltatory conduction | m/s |
| $f_{HR}$ | $f_{HR} = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{60 \text{ s/min}}{RR \text{ (s)}}$ | Heart rate from RR interval | bpm |
| $Q_{10}$ | $Q_{10} = \left( \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} \right)^{10/(T_2 - T_1)}$ | Temperature coefficient of rate | dimensionless |
| $\text{Time axis}$ | $t = \dfrac{\text{distance on paper (mm)}}{25 \text{ mm/s}}$ | Convert ECG mm to seconds | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU numerical problems, **always use the 61 mV form** of the Nernst equation at body temperature (310 K): $E = \dfrac{61}{z} \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_o}{[\text{ion}]_i} \right)$ mV.

## 2.7 Bioelectric Potentials — Clinical & Engineering Relevance

- **Nernst & GHK equations** are foundational to designing **pace-makers**, **neural stimulators**, and **defibrillators**.
- Conduction velocity data underpins **nerve conduction studies (NCS)** and **EMG** diagnosis of demyelinating diseases (e.g., Multiple Sclerosis, Guillain-Barré).
- ECG (below) is the **most performed diagnostic test worldwide**.

---

## 2.8 ECG — Deep Theory

### 2.8.1 The Cardiac Conduction System

The heart contains specialized autorhythmic (pacemaker) cells that generate APs in a fixed sequence:

$$SA \text{ node} \rightarrow \text{Atrial walls} \rightarrow AV \text{ node} \rightarrow \text{Bundle of His} \rightarrow \text{Bundle branches} \rightarrow \text{Purkinje fibers} \rightarrow \text{Ventricular myocardium}$$

| Structure | Intrinsic Rate | Function |
|---|---|---|
| SA node (pacemaker) | 60–100 bpm | Initiates each heartbeat |
| AV node | 40–60 bpm | Delays signal ≈ 0.1 s (atrial contraction completes) |
| Bundle of His & branches | 25–40 bpm | Rapid conduction to ventricles |
| Purkinje fibers | < 25 bpm | Distribute signal to ventricular muscle |

### 2.8.2 ECG Leads — The 12-Lead System

A **lead** is *not* a wire — it is a *vectorial view* of the heart's electrical activity from a particular angle.

**I. Limb Leads — Frontal Plane (Einthoven's Triangle)**

Three **bipolar** leads form Einthoven's triangle:
- **Lead I:** LA (+) − RA (−)
- **Lead II:** LL (+) − RA (−)  *← most commonly monitored*
- **Lead III:** LL (+) − LA (−)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Einthoven's Law:** $I + III = II$ (at any instant, algebraically). This must hold for a valid ECG and is used as a calibration check.

Three **augmented unipolar** leads (Goldberger):
- **aVR** (augmented Vector Right): explores right shoulder.
- **aVL** (augmented Vector Left): explores left shoulder.
- **aVF** (augmented Vector Foot): explores inferior wall.

**II. Precordial (Chest) Leads — Horizontal Plane (Wilson)**

Six unipolar leads placed on the chest wall: **V1, V2, V3, V4, V5, V6**.

Standard positions: V1 at 4th intercostal space right sternal border; V4 at 5th ICS mid-clavicular line; V2 opposite V1; V3 between V2 and V4; V5 anterior axillary line (same level as V4); V6 mid-axillary line.

### 2.8.3 ECG Waves and Intervals

| Wave / Interval | Origin (Physiology) | Duration | Amplitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization (SA → atria) | < 0.12 s | < 2.5 mm |
| **PR segment** | Conduction delay at AV node | — | Isoelectric |
| **PR interval** | Start of P to start of QRS | 0.12–0.20 s | — |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | 0.06–0.10 s | 5–25 mm |
| **ST segment** | Ventricular plateau (all cells depolarized) | 0.08–0.12 s | Isoelectric |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 0.16 s | < 5 mm |
| **QT interval** | Total ventricular systole (electrical) | 0.36–0.44 s | — |
| **U wave** (sometimes) | Late repolarization of Purkinje / papillary muscle | small | < 1 mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Atrial repolarization is hidden inside the QRS complex** (called the **Ta wave**), because ventricular mass is far larger.

### 2.8.4 The Heart's Mean Electrical Axis

The **mean electrical axis** is the average direction of depolarization in the frontal plane, computed from the net QRS area. Normal axis: **−30° to +90°**. Determined using the **quadrant method** from Leads I and aVF.

| Lead I | Lead aVF | Axis |
|---|---|---|
| + | + | Normal |
| + | − | Left axis deviation |
| − | + | Right axis deviation |
| − | − | Extreme axis deviation |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Equation (for an Ion at Equilibrium)

The Nernst equation arises from the **electrochemical equilibrium** between two opposing forces:
1. **Diffusion force** (concentration gradient) trying to drive the ion down its gradient.
2. **Electrical force** (potential difference) trying to drive the ion toward the oppositely charged side.

**Step 1 — Free energy change (chemical).** For an ion moving from inside to outside, the chemical work is:

$$\Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_o}{[\text{ion}]_i}$$

**Step 2 — Free energy change (electrical).** For an ion of valence $z$ moving across a potential difference $E$ (outside − inside):

$$\Delta G_{elec} = z F E$$

**Step 3 — At electrochemical equilibrium**, $\Delta G_{total} = 0$:

$$RT \ln \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_o}{[\text{ion}]_i} + z F E = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $E$:**

$$E = -\dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_o}{[\text{ion}]_i} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_i}{[\text{ion}]_o}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to base-10 logarithm** (using $2.303 \log_{10} = \ln$):

$$E = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_i}{[\text{ion}]_o}$$

**Step 6 — At body temperature $T = 310$ K**, $\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \approx 61$ mV:

$$\boxed{\,E_{ion} = \dfrac{61}{z} \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{ion}]_i}{[\text{ion}]_o} \;\; \text{mV}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example — Nernst Potential for K⁺

Given:
- $[\text{K}^+]_i = 150$ mM (intracellular)
- $[\text{K}^+]_o = 5$ mM (extracellular)
- $z = +1$

**Step 1 — Substitute into Nernst equation:**

$$E_K = \dfrac{61}{+1} \log_{10} \dfrac{150}{5} \;\; \text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the ratio:**

$$E_K = 61 \times \log_{10}(30) \;\; \text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\log_{10}(30)$:**

$$\log_{10}(30) = \log_{10}(3) + \log_{10}(10) = 0.477 + 1.000 = 1.477$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$E_K = 61 \times 1.477 \approx 90.1 \;\; \text{mV}$$

**Step 5 — Apply sign (ratio > 1, ion positive, inside > outside):**

$$E_K \approx -90 \;\; \text{mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the textbook value, confirming the resting membrane is dominated by $K^+$ permeability.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Nernst Potential for Na⁺

Given:
- $[\text{Na}^+]_o = 145$ mM, $[\text{Na}^+]_i = 12$ mM, $z = +1$

**Step 1:**

$$E_{Na} = \dfrac{61}{1} \log_{10} \dfrac{12}{145} \;\; \text{mV}$$

**Step 2 — Ratio is < 1, so log is negative:**

$$\log_{10}(12/145) = \log_{10}(0.0828) = -1.082$$

**Step 3:**

$$E_{Na} = 61 \times (-1.082) \approx -66 \;\; \text{mV}$$

**Sign convention correction:** Using the alternate form $E = \dfrac{61}{z} \log_{10}\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_o}{[\text{ion}]_i}$ gives $E_{Na} = +66$ mV. This is the **Nernst potential for Na⁺**, the driving force that depolarizes the cell during an AP.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Conduction Velocity in an Unmyelinated Axon

Starting from **Hodgkin's cable-equation model** for a uniform axon, the local circuit current density $I_a$ in the axoplasm is governed by the **telegraph equation**:

$$\lambda^2 \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - \tau \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} - V = 0$$

For a propagating wave moving with constant velocity $v$, substitute $V(x,t) = f(x - vt)$ and solve for $v$. After dimensional analysis, the **conduction velocity is** (Hodgkin 1954):

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{d}{4 R_i c_m^2 R_m^2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{K}$$

Reducing to scaling laws:
- $v \propto \sqrt{d}$ (unmyelinated) — **square-root dependence** on diameter.
- $v$ depends on $\sqrt{1 / (R_i c_m^2 R_m^2)}$.

**Physical interpretation:**
- ↑ diameter $d$ → ↓ $R_i$ (more cytoplasm) → ↑ $v$.
- ↑ membrane capacitance $c_m$ → more charge needed → ↓ $v$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Conduction Velocity in a Myelinated Axon (Rushton / Huxley)

For a myelinated fiber, AP regenerates only at Nodes of Ranvier. The internodal length is $L$, and internodal resistance / capacitance are dominated by myelin.

The current at each node must charge the capacitance of the adjacent internode. The time to charge is:

$$t_{charge} \propto R_{node} \cdot C_{internode} \propto R_{node} \cdot (c_m \cdot \pi d \cdot L)$$

For **optimal speed**, internodal length $L$ should be proportional to diameter $d$ (Rushton, 1951):

$$L_{optimal} \approx 100 \cdot d$$

Then the conduction velocity becomes:

$$v = \dfrac{L_{optimal}}{t_{charge}} \propto d$$

Thus, for myelinated fibers: $\boxed{v \propto d}$ — **linear** dependence on diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why the largest myelinated fibers (≈ 20 µm) can conduct at ≈ 120 m/s, while unmyelinated fibers of the same diameter conduct at only ≈ 2 m/s.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Example — Conduction Velocity Using $Q_{10}$

A nerve conducts at $v_1 = 30$ m/s at $T_1 = 27 °C$. Find $v_2$ at $T_2 = 37 °C$ (body temperature), given $Q_{10} = 1.6$.

**Step 1 — Apply the $Q_{10}$ relation:**

$$Q_{10} = \left( \dfrac{v_2}{v_1} \right)^{10 / (T_2 - T_1)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$1.6 = \left( \dfrac{v_2}{30} \right)^{10 / (37 - 27)} = \left( \dfrac{v_2}{30} \right)^{1.0}$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$v_2 = 30 \times 1.6 = 48 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Higher temperature → faster conduction. This is why limbs feel "numb" in cold (slower nerve conduction) and why hypothermia is dangerous in surgery.

---

## 3.7 Numerical Example — Heart Rate from ECG Paper

A patient's ECG trace shows **one R–R interval = 22 mm** on standard paper (25 mm/s).

**Step 1 — Convert mm to seconds:**

$$RR = \dfrac{22 \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ mm/s}} = 0.88 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Heart rate (bpm):**

$$HR = \dfrac{60}{RR} = \dfrac{60}{0.88} \approx 68 \text{ bpm}$$

> This is a **normal sinus rhythm**.

**Quick KTU reference table:**

| RR (mm) | RR (s) | HR (bpm) |
|---|---|---|
| 50 | 2.0 | 30 |
| 40 | 1.6 | 37.5 |
| 30 | 1.2 | 50 |
| 25 | 1.0 | 60 |
| 20 | 0.8 | 75 |
| 15 | 0.6 | 100 |
| 10 | 0.4 | 150 |

---

## 3.8 Numerical Example — Einthoven's Law Verification

A patient has the following QRS amplitudes (mV) measured simultaneously:
- Lead I: +0.8 mV
- Lead III: −0.2 mV
- Lead II: +0.6 mV

**Check:** $I + III = 0.8 + (-0.2) = 0.6$ mV $= II$ ✓

The trace is internally consistent; no lead-failure or electrode fault.

---

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Governing Equations

| # | Equation | Use | Key Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{61}{z} \log_{10}\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_i}{[\text{ion}]_o}$ | Nernst (single ion) | $z, [\text{ion}]$ |
| 2 | $V_m^{GHK} = 61 \log_{10}\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}$ | Multi-ion resting potential | $P_{ion}$ (permeabilities) |
| 3 | $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_i}$ | Length constant | $r_m, r_i$ |
| 4 | $\tau = r_m c_m$ | Membrane time constant | $r_m, c_m$ |
| 5 | $v \propto \sqrt{d}$ | Unmyelinated | $d$ |
| 6 | $v \propto d$ | Myelinated | $d$ |
| 7 | $Q_{10} = (R_2/R_1)^{10/(T_2 - T_1)}$ | Temperature effect | $R, T$ |
| 8 | $HR = 60 / RR$ (s) | ECG heart rate | RR |
| 9 | $I + III = II$ | Einthoven's law | Lead amplitudes |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Action Potential Propagation

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional architecture** of AP propagation, highlighting the local circuit current, threshold detection, ion-channel kinetics, and the regeneration cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Trigger Zone Axon Hillock] --> A1[Stimulus Reaches Threshold minus 55 mV]
    A1 --> A2[Voltage Gated Na Channels Open]
    A2 --> A3[Na Influx Membrane Depolarizes to plus 30 mV]
    A3 --> A4[Local Circuit Current Spreads to Adjacent Region]
    A4 --> A5{Adjacent Region Above Threshold}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[New AP Generated Here]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Signal Decays Electrotonically]
    A6 --> A3
    A3 --> A8[K Efflux Repolarization Phase]
    A8 --> A9[Na K Pump Restores Ionic Gradients]
    A9 --> A10[Refractory Period ends Membrane Ready]
```

## 4.2 Comparison: Continuous vs Saltatory Conduction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UM[Unmyelinated Axon Continuous Conduction]
        U1[Segment 1 AP] --> U2[Segment 2 AP]
        U2 --> U3[Segment 3 AP]
        U3 --> U4[Segment 4 AP]
        U4 --> U5[Segment 5 AP]
    end
    subgraph M[Myelinated Axon Saltatory Conduction]
        N1[Node 1 AP] -.-> N2[Node 2 AP]
        N2 -.-> N3[Node 3 AP]
        N3 -.-> N4[Node 4 AP]
        I1[Internode Insulated Myelin] -.- N1
        I2[Internode Insulated Myelin] -.- N2
        I3[Internode Insulated Myelin] -.- N3
        I4[Internode Insulated Myelin] -.- N4
    end
```

## 4.3 ECG Block Architecture — Cardiac Conduction System to Waveform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[SA Node Pacemaker 60 to 100 bpm] --> P[P Wave Atrial Depolarization]
    P --> AV[AV Node Delay approx 100 ms]
    AV --> BH[Bundle of His]
    BH --> BB[Bundle Branches Left and Right]
    BB --> PU[Purkinje Fibers]
    PU --> Q[Q Wave Initial Septal Depolarization]
    Q --> R[R Wave Main Ventricular Mass]
    R --> S2[S Wave Late Basal Depolarization]
    S2 --> ST[ST Segment Isoelectric Plateau]
    ST --> T[T Wave Ventricular Repolarization]
```

## 4.4 ECG Lead System (Einthoven + Augmented + Precordial)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LP[Limb Leads Frontal Plane] --> BL[Bipolar Einthoven Triangle]
    LP --> AL[Augmented Unipolar Goldberger]
    LP --> CP[Chest Leads Horizontal Plane Wilson]
    BL --> L1[Lead I LA minus RA]
    BL --> L2[Lead II LL minus RA]
    BL --> L3[Lead III LL minus LA]
    AL --> aVR
    AL --> aVL
    AL --> aVF
    CP --> V1
    CP --> V2
    CP --> V3
    CP --> V4
    CP --> V5
    CP --> V6
    L1 -- Einthoven Law I plus III equals II --> L2
    L2 -- equals --> L3
```

    P1[1 Small Box 1 mm by 1 mm] --> P1T[Equals 0.04 s by 0.1 mV]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define resting membrane potential and action potential. Mention their typical values.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP):** The steady-state potential difference across the cell membrane when the cell is not excited. Maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump and K⁺ leak channels. Typical value: **−70 mV** in neurons, **−90 mV** in cardiac ventricular cells (inside negative).
- **Action Potential (AP):** A rapid, transient, all-or-nothing reversal of the membrane potential in response to a stimulus above threshold, caused by sequential opening of voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels. Peak value: **+30 to +40 mV**, duration: **1–2 ms** in neurons, **200–300 ms** in cardiac cells.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is saltatory conduction? Why is it faster than continuous conduction?**

**Model Answer:**
Saltatory conduction is the process by which an action potential **"jumps" from one Node of Ranvier to the next** along a myelinated axon. The myelin sheath acts as a high-resistance, low-capacitance insulator, so the AP is regenerated only at the nodes.
It is faster than continuous conduction because:
1. Only the **nodes** (not every membrane segment) need to depolarize.
2. Myelin **reduces membrane capacitance** and **increases membrane resistance**, so the local-circuit current spreads further per node.
3. Conduction velocity scales **linearly with diameter** ($v \propto d$) rather than as $\sqrt{d}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**Explain the propagation of an action potential along a nerve fiber. Compare continuous and saltatory conduction. Discuss the factors affecting conduction velocity.**

#### (a) Mechanism of Propagation — 7 Marks

1. **Initiation:** A stimulus depolarizes the membrane to threshold (≈ −55 mV) at the trigger zone.
2. **Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open:** Na⁺ flows into the cell, reversing the local potential to ≈ +30 mV.
3. **Local circuit currents** flow between the depolarized (active) region and the adjacent polarized (resting) region, **passively depolarizing** the next segment.
4. When the next segment reaches threshold, **its Na⁺ channels open**, regenerating a full AP there.
5. The process repeats → **self-propagating wave** that travels without decrement.
6. The previous segment undergoes **Na⁺ channel inactivation and K⁺ efflux (repolarization)**, entering the **refractory period** — this ensures **unidirectional** propagation (toward unexcited membrane).
7. **Orthodromic** conduction = forward (physiological); **antidromic** = backward (occurs only experimentally).

#### (b) Continuous vs Saltatory Conduction & Factors — 7 Marks

| Feature | Continuous (Unmyelinated) | Saltatory (Myelinated) |
|---|---|---|
| Myelin | Absent | Present |
| Site of regeneration | Every membrane segment | Only at Nodes of Ranvier |
| Velocity | Slow (1–2 m/s) | Fast (up to 120 m/s) |
| Capacitance | High | Low (insulated) |
| Energy use | High (lots of Na⁺/K⁺ pumping) | Low |
| Length constant λ | Small | Large |

**Factors affecting conduction velocity:**
- **Diameter** of the axon (larger → faster; $v \propto \sqrt{d}$ unmyelinated, $v \propto d$ myelinated).
- **Myelination** (presence of myelin dramatically increases velocity).
- **Temperature** (higher $T$ → faster; $Q_{10} \approx 1.5$).
- **Membrane capacitance** (lower → faster).
- **Membrane resistance** (higher → longer λ, faster).

**Equations:**

$$\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}, \qquad \tau = r_m c_m$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)
