**With a neat block diagram, explain the 12-lead ECG system. Describe the origin and significance of each ECG wave. State the normal time durations.**

#### (a) 12-Lead ECG System — 7 Marks

A **lead** is a *vectorial view* of cardiac electrical activity from a specific pair of electrodes. There are **12 standard leads** in clinical ECG:

**1. Three Bipolar Limb Leads (Einthoven's Triangle — frontal plane):**
- **Lead I** = LA − RA
- **Lead II** = LL − RA
- **Lead III** = LL − LA
- **Einthoven's Law:** $I + III = II$ at every instant.

**2. Three Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger — frontal plane):**
- **aVR** — right shoulder (−30°)
- **aVL** — left shoulder (−30° to −60°)
- **aVF** — foot (+90°)

**3. Six Precordial (Chest) Leads (Wilson — horizontal plane):**
- V1 (4th ICS, right sternal border), V2 (4th ICS, left sternal border), V3 (between V2 and V4), V4 (5th ICS, mid-clavicular line), V5 (anterior axillary line), V6 (mid-axillary line).

Block diagram: see Section 4.4.

#### (b) ECG Waves — Origin and Significance — 7 Marks

| Wave / Interval | Origin | Significance | Normal Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization (SA → atria) | Atrial contraction follows | < 0.12 s |
| **PR interval** | Atrial depolarization + AV nodal delay | Measures AV conduction time | 0.12–0.20 s |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | Triggers ventricular contraction | 0.06–0.10 s |
| **ST segment** | All ventricular cells in plateau (phase 2) | Isoelectric; ST elevation/depression indicates ischemia | 0.08–0.12 s |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | Restoring ventricular polarity | 0.16 s |
| **QT interval** | Total ventricular electrical activity | Prolonged QT → risk of arrhythmia (Torsades) | 0.36–0.44 s |
| **U wave** | Late repolarization of Purkinje/papillary | Often seen in hypokalemia | small |

**ECG paper calibration:** 25 mm/s speed, 10 mm/mV amplitude.
**Heart rate calculation:** $HR = \dfrac{60}{RR \text{ in seconds}}$ bpm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Many students confuse **"lead" with "electrode"**. A lead is a *view* (vector), not a wire.
> 2. Do **not** write "Lead I = LA + RA"; it is **LA − RA** (positive electrode − negative electrode).
> 3. The **PR interval** is from the *start of P* to the *start of QRS*, **not** to the peak of R.
> 4. **Atrial repolarization (Ta wave)** is hidden inside the QRS — students often forget this.
> 5. **Einthoven's Law** must be stated and can be tested numerically.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **RMP** ≈ −70 mV; **AP peak** ≈ +30 mV; **Threshold** ≈ −55 mV; **Duration** ≈ 1–2 ms (nerve).
- **Nernst equation (37 °C):** $E = \dfrac{61}{z} \log_{10}\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_i}{[\text{ion}]_o}$ mV.
- **GHK equation** gives the *real* resting potential considering Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻ simultaneously.
- **Local-circuit currents** drive propagation; AP is **regenerated, not attenuated**.
- **Continuous conduction** (unmyelinated): $v \propto \sqrt{d}$, slow.
- **Saltatory conduction** (myelinated): $v \propto d$, fast, energy efficient.
- **Length constant** $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m / r_i}$; **Time constant** $\tau = r_m c_m$.
- **Higher $Q_{10}$** → temperature-sensitive conduction.
- **ECG = summed cardiac electrical activity**; **Einthoven** invented it; **paper 25 mm/s, 10 mm/mV**.
- **12 leads:** 3 bipolar (I, II, III) + 3 augmented (aVR, aVL, aVF) + 6 precordial (V1–V6).
- **Einthoven's Law:** $I + III = II$ (always true — used as a calibration check).
- **P wave** → atrial depolarization; **QRS** → ventricular depolarization; **T wave** → ventricular repolarization.
- **PR interval** = 0.12–0.20 s (AV conduction); **QRS** = 0.06–0.10 s; **QT** = 0.36–0.44 s.
- **Heart rate:** $HR = 60 / RR(\text{s})$ bpm.
- **Atrial repolarization (Ta)** is **hidden inside QRS** (mass much larger).
- **Mean electrical axis:** normal = −30° to +90°; determined from Lead I and aVF.
- **Clinical pearls:** MS (demyelination) → slow conduction; hyperkalemia → peaked T wave; prolonged QT → Torsades risk.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EOG and EGG. Electrode Theory - Nernst Relation. Bio Potential Electrodes - Microelectrodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET612 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** EOG and EGG. Electrode Theory - Nernst Relation. Bio Potential Electrodes - Microelectrodes # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Electrooculography (EOG) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** > *Electrooculography (EOG)* is the technique of recording the standing potential of the eye (the *corneo-retinal potential*) by placing surface electrodes on the skin around the orbit. The eye behaves as a fixed electrical dipole with the **cornea positively charged (~+0.4 to +1.0 mV)** relative to the **negatively charged retina**, and this dipole rotates with eye movement, producing measurable voltage changes. - **Recorded Signal:** **0.5 mV – 1.0 mV** (Standing potential of the eye) - **Frequency Band:** **DC to ~10 Hz** - **Primary Clinical Use:** Diagnosis of *Best Disease*, assessment of Retinal Pigment Epithelium (RPE) function, sleep studies, eye-movement tracking in HCI (Human-Computer Interface). > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "The Battery Inside Your Eye":** > Think of the eyeball as a tiny biological battery. The front of the eye (cornea) is the **positive terminal**, and the back (retina) is the **negative terminal**. This battery always stays on — it has a steady voltage even when your eyes are still. When you move your eyes left or right, you are physically **rotating this tiny battery**. Electrodes glued to the skin near your eyes can detect the rotation because the positive terminal comes closer to or moves away from each electrode, just like waving a magnet near a Hall sensor. The voltage change tells us exactly how your eyes moved. ## 1.2 Electrogastrography (EGG) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** > *Electrogastrography (EGG)* is a non-invasive method of recording the myoelectrical activity of the gastric smooth muscle (stomach) using cutaneous electrodes placed on the abdominal surface over the stomach. The dominant signal is the *gastric slow wave*, generated by the Interstitial Cells of Cajal (ICC). - **Normal Gastric Slow-Wave Frequency:** **0.05 Hz (3 cycles per minute)** - **Signal Amplitude:** **100 μV – 500 μV** (surface recording) - **Abnormal Frequencies:** - *Bradygastria:* < 2 cpm - *Tachygastria:* 4 – 9 cpm - *Arrhythmia:* No dominant frequency - **Band-Pass Filter Range:** **0.033 Hz – 0.25 Hz** (typical) > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "Listening to the Stomach's Pacemaker":** > Imagine the stomach has a built-in pacemaker — a tiny cluster of cells called the ICC that sends a slow, rhythmic electrical wave across the stomach wall every 20 seconds (3 times per minute) to coordinate digestion. This wave is far too weak for you to feel, but with sensitive electrodes placed on the belly, we can "listen in" — just as you might hear a distant drum through a wall. If the drum beats too slow (bradygastria) or too fast (tachygastria), it indicates the stomach's pacemaker is malfunctioning, often linked to nausea, gastroparesis, or motion sickness. ## 1.3 Bio-Potential Electrodes > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** > A *bio-potential electrode* is a transducer that converts the ionic current flow inside the body (carried by ions such as Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺) into an electronic current flow in the measuring instrument (carried by electrons) by means of a redox (oxidation-reduction) electrochemical reaction at the *electrode-electrolyte interface*. The general classification is: - **Macro (Surface) Electrodes** — e.g., Ag/AgCl, plate electrodes - **Needle Electrodes** — penetrate the skin - **Microelectrodes** — record from single cells (focus of this module) ## 1.4 Microelectrodes > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** > *Microelectrodes* are bio-potential electrodes with extremely small tip dimensions (typically **< 10 μm**) used to record electrical activity from single cells, neurons, or muscle fibers. They exhibit very high source impedance (often **10 MΩ – 100 MΩ**) and therefore require high-input-impedance amplifiers. Two principal classes: - **Metal Microelectrodes** — e.g., Tungsten, Platinum-Iridium, Stainless Steel - **Micropipette (Glass) Microelectrodes** — filled with an electrolyte (commonly **3 M KCl**) > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "A Needle Inside a Neuron":** > Imagine a single nerve cell is the size of a soccer ball. To record the tiny electrical buzz happening inside it (a few millivolts for about 1 millisecond), you need a probe so fine that it doesn't damage the cell — like inserting a single human hair through the cell wall. That's essentially what a microelectrode is: an ultra-thin needle with a tip finer than a red blood cell (~5 μm). Micropipette versions are like tiny glass straws filled with salt water (KCl solution) that act as a salt bridge from inside the cell to your amplifier. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 EOG — Operational Principle The eye behaves as a *fixed dipole* with a standing *corneo-retinal potential* of approximately **+0.4 mV to +1.0 mV** (cornea positive with respect to retina). **Why does a dipole form?** - The Retinal Pigment Epithelium (RPE) maintains a *transepithelial potential* through active ionic pumping (especially **K⁺**). - This creates a stable **+5 mV to +6 mV** potential in the subretinal space relative to the choroid. - Light absorption by the photoreceptors modulates this potential → basis of the *electroretinogram (ERG)*, not the EOG. - EOG specifically captures the **DC standing potential** that rotates with eye position. **How electrodes detect rotation:** 1. Five electrodes are typically used: one above, one below, one lateral to each eye, and a reference (e.g., forehead). 2. As the eye moves **horizontally** to the right, the cornea approaches the right electrode → that electrode becomes more positive. 3. The difference between left and right electrodes is the **horizontal EOG channel**. 4. A similar vertical channel captures up/down movements. **Engineering Use Cases:** - *Sleep Studies (Polysomnography):* EOG is mandatory to detect REM (Rapid Eye Movement) sleep. - *Ophthalmology:* Diagnosis of Best Vitelliform Macular Dystrophy (Arden Ratio test). - *Biometrics & HCI:* Low-cost eye-tracking for gaming, AR/VR. ## 2.2 EGG — Operational Principle The stomach's smooth muscle has three layers. A *pacemaker* region in the upper body of the stomach (near the greater curvature) generates slow waves that propagate toward the pylorus. **Why does a slow wave exist?** - The *Interstitial Cells of Cajal (ICC)* are pacemaker cells that generate rhythmic depolarizations via **IP₃-mediated Ca²⁺ release** and **slow Ca²⁺-activated Cl⁻ efflux**. - Normal slow-wave frequency in humans: **3 cpm (0.05 Hz)**. - The slow wave itself usually does not cause contraction — it sets the *maximum* rate of contraction. - An Action Potential (spike) rides on top of the slow wave when the threshold is exceeded → triggers muscle contraction. **How is the EGG recorded?** 1. **3 to 4 surface Ag/AgCl electrodes** placed on the abdomen along the stomach axis. 2. Signal is amplified (~1500×) and band-pass filtered (**0.033 Hz – 0.25 Hz**). 3. *Running Spectral Analysis* (FFT of sliding 1–4 min windows) reveals dominant frequency. 4. A *normal* EGG is dominated by **3 cpm** activity; deviations indicate gastric dysrhythmia. **Engineering Use Cases:** - *Gastroenterology:* Non-invasive assessment of gastric motility disorders (gastroparesis, functional dyspepsia). - *Nausea detection* (tachygastria is strongly correlated with motion sickness and chemotherapy-induced nausea). - *Post-operative monitoring* of gastric recovery. ## 2.3 Electrode Theory — The Electrode-Electrolyte Interface When a metal is immersed in an electrolyte, an **electrical double layer** forms at the interface, comprising: 1. *Inner Helmholtz Plane (IHP)* — specifically adsorbed ions 2. *Outer Helmholtz Plane (OHP)* — solvated non-adsorbed ions 3. *Diffuse (Gouy-Chapman) Layer* — thermally distributed ions This interface acts as a **capacitor** (Helmholtz capacitance, C_H ~ **10–40 μF/cm²**) in parallel with a **faradaic resistance** (R_f). The interface also gives rise to a **half-cell potential** governed by the **Nernst Equation**. ## 2.4 Nernst Equation — The Cornerstone of Bioelectrode Theory > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Nernst Equation** gives the *equilibrium reduction potential* of an electrode as a function of the ionic concentrations in the adjacent electrolyte. It is the thermodynamic foundation for understanding bio-potential electrodes and the resting membrane potential of cells. The general form of the Nernst equation is: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q)$$ For a temperature of **37 °C (310 K)** — the *physiological standard* used in biomedical engineering — the constant evaluates as: $$\frac{2.303 \cdot R \cdot T}{F} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$ This gives the **physiological Nernst equation**: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{61.5}{n} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}\right) \text{ mV}$$ Or, for a simple metal-electrode reaction (M^n+ + ne⁻ ⇌ M): $$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{61.5}{n} \log_{10}\left([M^{n+}]\right) \text{ mV}$$ **Real-World Utility:** - Predicts the *half-cell potential* of Ag/AgCl, calomel, and other reference electrodes. - Underlies the *Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation* for the resting membrane potential of a neuron. - Determines the *open-circuit voltage* of any bio-potential electrode pair (E_cell = E_cathode − E_anode). ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these equations for KTU University Exam questions on this topic.** | # | Equation / Quantity | Mathematical Form | Typical Value / Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Nernst Equation (general) | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q)$ | Volts (V) | Half-cell potential | | 2 | Nernst Equation (37 °C, base 10) | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{61.5}{n} \log_{10}(Q)$ mV | mV | Physiological bioelectrodes | | 3 | Nernst Equation (cell, two half cells) | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}}^{\circ} - E_{\text{anode}}^{\circ} - \dfrac{61.5}{n} \log_{10}(Q_{\text{cell}})$ mV | mV | Full electrode cell | | 4 | Corneo-retinal Standing Potential | $V_{\text{CR}}$ | **0.4 to 1.0 mV** | EOG | | 5 | EOG Frequency Band | $f_{\text{EOG}}$ | **DC to 10 Hz** | EOG amplifier design | | 6 | Gastric Slow-Wave Frequency | $f_{\text{gastric}}$ | **0.05 Hz (3 cpm)** | EGG | | 7 | EGG Amplitude (surface) | $V_{\text{EGG}}$ | **100 to 500 μV** | EGG amplifier design | | 8 | EGG Band-pass Filter | $f_{\text{LP}} - f_{\text{HP}}$ | **0.033 Hz – 0.25 Hz** | EGG signal conditioning | | 9 | Helmholtz Capacitance | $C_H = \dfrac{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | **10–40 μF/cm²** | Electrode-skin interface | | 10 | Microelectrode Tip Impedance | $Z_{\text{tip}}$ | **1 MΩ – 100 MΩ** | Amplifier input selection | | 11 | Micropipette Tip Diameter | $d_{\text{tip}}$ | **< 10 μm** (often ~1 μm) | Intracellular recording | | 12 | Microelectrode Capacitance (cylinder) | $C = \dfrac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(r_{\text{outer}}/r_{\text{inner}})}$ | pF range | High-frequency response | | 13 | Cell Constant (Nernst, K⁺) | $E_{K} = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}\right)$ mV | mV | Neuron resting potential | | 14 | Boltzmann Constant | $k$ | **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | Statistical mechanics | | 15 | Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96485 C/mol** | Electrochemistry | | 16 | Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Thermodynamics | | 17 | Body Temperature (physiological) | $T$ | **310 K (37 °C)** | Nernst simplification | ## 2.6 Microelectrodes — Engineering Detail **Metal Microelectrodes:** - *Material:* Tungsten (W), Platinum-Iridium (Pt-Ir), Stainless Steel. - *Construction:* Solid metal shaft electrolytically etched to a fine tip, then insulated (e.g., with parylene-C or glass) except at the apex. - *Impedance:* **0.5 MΩ – 5 MΩ** at 1 kHz (for typical 1–5 μm tips). - *Application:* Extracellular neural recording, deep-brain stimulation, EMG (concentric needle). **Micropipette (Glass) Microelectrodes:** - *Construction:* A glass capillary (borosilicate) is heated and pulled to produce two fine-tipped pipettes. The tip is filled with an electrolyte (**3 M KCl** is standard; **K-citrate** or **K-acetate** for ion-sensitive variants). - *Impedance:* **10 MΩ – 100 MΩ** at 1 kHz (depends on tip diameter). - *Application:* *Intracellular* recording of resting and action potentials, ion-selective measurement, patch-clamp precursor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the high impedance matters (KTU favourite):** > Because the microelectrode's source impedance can be comparable to or larger than the input impedance of a standard op-amp, we must use a **unity-gain buffer / follower** with input impedance **> 10¹² Ω** (FET or electrometer-grade op-amp, e.g., **AD549, TL072, OPA129**) placed *as close to the electrode as possible* to prevent signal attenuation and high-frequency roll-off. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation **Starting Point — Chemical Thermodynamics:** For a general reversible electrochemical half-reaction: $$a \cdot A + b \cdot B + n \cdot e^{-} \rightleftharpoons c \cdot C + d \cdot D$$ The change in **Gibbs Free Energy** at any state is given by: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln(Q_{\text{r}})$$ where: - $\Delta G$ = Gibbs free energy change under non-standard conditions (J/mol) - $\Delta G^{\circ}$ = Gibbs free energy change under standard conditions (J/mol) - $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = Absolute temperature (K) - $Q_r$ = Reaction quotient, equal to $\dfrac{[C]^c [D]^d}{[A]^a [B]^b}$ (dimensionless, for solutes) **Step 1 — Apply the Nernst Condition:** The maximum non-expansion work obtainable from an electrochemical cell equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy: $$W_{\max} = -\Delta G = nFE$$ where $E$ is the cell potential and $n$ is the number of electrons transferred. **Step 2 — Substitute into the Gibbs Expression:** $$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln(Q_{\text{r}})$$ **Step 3 — Divide through by $-nF$ to isolate E:** $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q_{\text{r}})$$ This is the **Nernst Equation** in its natural-logarithm form. ✓ **Step 4 — Conversion to Base-10 Logarithm:** Using the identity $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10}(Q_{\text{r}})$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the Constant at Physiological Temperature (T = 310 K):** $$\frac{2.303 \cdot R \cdot T}{F} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485}$$ Let us expand this term by term: $$\begin{aligned} 2.303 \times 8.314 &= 19.147 \;\; \text{J/(mol·K)} \\[4pt] 19.147 \times 310 &= 5935.6 \;\; \text{J/mol} \\[4pt] 5935.6 \div 96485 &= 0.06152 \;\; \text{V} \\[4pt] &= 61.52 \;\; \text{mV} \end{aligned}$$ So at body temperature: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{61.5}{n} \log_{10}(Q_{\text{r}}) \quad [\text{in mV}]$$ **Step 6 — The Nernst Equation for the Potassium Equilibrium Potential (used in Neurons):** For the reaction $K^+_{\text{outside}} \rightleftharpoons K^+_{\text{inside}}$ (with concentration gradient maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump), the equilibrium potential is: $$E_{K} = 61.5 \, \log_{10}\left(\frac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}\right) \;\; \text{mV}$$ **Sample Numerical Evaluation:** Given: $[K^+]_o = 5 \text{ mM}$ and $[K^+]_i = 140 \text{ mM}$. $$\begin{aligned} E_{K} &= 61.5 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{5}{140}\right) \;\; \text{mV} \\[4pt] &= 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.0357) \;\; \text{mV} \\[4pt] &= 61.5 \times (-1.447) \;\; \text{mV} \\[4pt] &\approx -89.0 \;\; \text{mV} \end{aligned}$$ This matches the well-known *Nernst potential for K⁺* in a typical neuron (≈ −90 mV). ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation of the EOG Output Voltage **Setup:** - The eye is a dipole with the cornea at potential $+V_{\text{CR}}$ and the retina at $0$ V. - Two electrodes are placed on the **left** and **right** of the eye, at equal angles $\theta$ from the dipole axis. - The dipole is at distance $r$ from each electrode. **Geometric Voltage at Electrode:** The potential at a point at distance $r$ and angle $\theta$ from a dipole of moment $\vec{p}$ is: $$V = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{p \cos\theta}{r^2}$$ **Difference Between Right and Left Electrodes:** When the eye rotates by angle $\alpha$ (from straight-ahead), the angle to one electrode becomes $\theta - \alpha$ and the other becomes $\theta + \alpha$ (or similar geometric arrangement). For small angles and symmetric placement, the differential signal simplifies to: $$V_{\text{EOG}} = k \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$ where $k$ is a calibration constant (in mV/degree), and $\sin(\alpha)$ represents the horizontal eye position. **Engineering Implication:** - EOG output is **non-linear** with eye angle at extremes (> ±30°). - A *linearization* step (arc-sine or polynomial correction) is applied in software for accurate gaze tracking. - Typical linear range: **± 30°** horizontal gaze. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Microelectrode Cable Equation (Simplified) A microelectrode can be modelled as a **distributed RC line** (transmission line). The basic *equivalent circuit* is: $$Z_{\text{electrode}} = R_{\text{tip}} \parallel \left( \frac{1}{j \omega C_{\text{tip}}} \right)$$ For a long insulated shaft of length $L$, outer radius $r_o$, inner radius $r_i$, the **distributed capacitance per unit length** is: $$C' = \frac{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(r_o / r_i)} \;\; \text{F/m}$$ The total capacitance is then: $$C_{\text{total}} = C' \cdot L$$ The **3-dB cutoff frequency** of the electrode (when driving an amplifier of input capacitance $C_{\text{in}}$) is: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{\text{tip}} (C_{\text{total}} + C_{\text{in}})}$$ **Engineering Implication:** - High-impedance microelectrodes (e.g., 50 MΩ) severely attenuate fast neural spikes (≈ 1 kHz) if the input capacitance is significant. - *Active probes* (head-stages) placed physically close to the electrode minimise $C_{\text{in}}$ and the cable length $L$. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Microelectrode Comparison | Parameter | Metal Microelectrode | Micropipette (Glass) Microelectrode | |---|---|---| | **Material** | W, Pt-Ir, Stainless Steel | Borosilicate glass + 3 M KCl | | **Tip Diameter** | 1 – 10 μm | 0.5 – 5 μm | | **Impedance at 1 kHz** | 0.5 – 5 MΩ | 10 – 100 MΩ | | **Recording Type** | Primarily *Extracellular* | Primarily *Intracellular* | | **DC Offset Stability** | Excellent | Drifts due to tip potential | | **Tip Potential** | Negligible | **± 5 mV to ± 30 mV** (KCl offsets) | | **Reusability** | Limited (sharpening needed) | Single-use (fragile) | | **Cost** | Moderate | Low | | **Selectivity** | Records multi-unit activity | Records single-cell activity | | **Damage to Tissue** | Moderate | Low (penetrates cell membrane) | | **Typical Use** | Neural prosthetics, DBS, EMG | Patch clamp, resting potential | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 EOG Measurement System — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[EYE DIPOLE
Cornea positive
Retina negative
Standing V = 0.4 to 1.0 mV] --> B[SKIN SURFACE
ELECTRODES
4 Ag/AgCl electrodes
around orbital rim] B --> C[INSTRUMENTATION
AMPLIFIER
Gain = 1000 to 5000
CMRR greater than 100 dB] C --> D[BAND PASS FILTER
0.1 Hz to 30 Hz
Removes DC drift and HF noise] D --> E[ADC
Sampling greater than 200 Hz
16 bit resolution] E --> F[DISPLAY AND STORAGE
PC monitor oscilloscope
Sleep study software] F --> G[CLINICAL OUTPUT
Horizontal EOG trace
Vertical EOG trace
REM detection] ``` ## 4.2 EGG Measurement System — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[STOMACH SLOW WAVE
ICC pacemaker
3 cycles per minute
0.05 Hz] --> A2[ABDOMINAL SURFACE
3 to 4 Ag/AgCl electrodes
placed along gastric axis] A2 --> A3[HIGH GAIN AMPLIFIER
Gain approximately 1500
Driven right leg] A3 --> A4[BAND PASS FILTER
0.033 Hz to 0.25 Hz
Removes respiratory and cardiac artifacts] A4 --> A5[ADC and PC INTERFACE
Sampling greater than 2 Hz] A5 --> A6[RUNNING SPECTRAL ANALYSIS
FFT of 1 to 4 min windows
Dominant frequency extraction] A6 --> A7[DIAGNOSTIC OUTPUT
Normal 3 cpm Brady Tachy Arrhythmia] ``` ## 4.3 Bioelectrode Half-Cell — Nernst Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR A[METAL ELECTRODE
e.g. Ag] --> B[ELECTRODE ELECTROLYTE INTERFACE
Helmholtz double layer
Capacitance 10 to 40 uF per cm2] B --> C[IONIC CHARGE TRANSFER
Ag to Ag positive plus e
redox reaction] C --> D[HALF CELL POTENTIAL
given by Nernst equation
E equals E zero minus 61.5 over n times log Q] D --> E[REFERENCE ELECTRODE
completes the cell
E cell equals E cathode minus E anode] ``` ## 4.4 Microelectrode Classification & Signal Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[BIO POTENTIAL ELECTRODES] --> B[MACRO SURFACE] A --> C[NEEDLE] A --> D[MICRO] B --> B1[Ag AgCl plate electrodes
ECG EEG EMG surface] C --> C1[Concentric needle EMG
Bipolar needle] D --> D1[METAL MICROELECTRODES
Tungsten Pt Ir
Extracellular recording] D --> D2[MICROPIPETTE GLASS MICROELECTRODES
3 M KCl filled
Intracellular recording] D1 --> E1[HIGH Z BUFFER
FET op amp
Impedance greater than 10 to the 12 ohms] D2 --> E2[HIGH Z BUFFER
Electrometer op amp
AD549 OPA129] E1 --> F1[AMPLIFIER and ADC] E2 --> F2[AMPLIFIER and ADC] F1 --> G1[COMPUTER ANALYSIS] F2 --> G2[COMPUTER ANALYSIS] ``` ## 4.5 Nernst Equation Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CONCENTRATION DIFFERENCE
Ion gradient across membrane] --> B[NERNST EQUATION
E equals E zero minus 61.5 over n times log Q] B --> C[EQUILIBRIUM POTENTIAL
For K plus approx negative 90 mV
For Na plus approx positive 60 mV
For Cl negative 70 mV] C --> D[GOLDMAN HODGKIN KATZ EQUATION
Weighted sum of permeabilities
Resting membrane potential negative 70 mV] D --> E[ACTION POTENTIAL GENERATION
Neuron and muscle physiology] B --> F[BIO POTENTIAL ELECTRODE
Half cell potential prediction
Ag AgCl approx positive 222 mV] F --> G[OFFSET CANCELLATION
Instrumentation amplifier design] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]** *Define Electrooculography (EOG). Mention the origin of the signal and any two clinical applications.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Electrooculography (EOG) is a technique used to record the standing potential of the eye by placing surface electrodes on the skin around the orbit. - **Origin of the signal (1 mark):** The eye behaves as an electric dipole — the cornea is positive and the retina is negative, producing a *corneo-retinal standing potential* of approximately **0.4 to 1.0 mV**. Movement of the eye rotates this dipole and produces measurable voltage changes between skin electrodes. - **Clinical applications (1 mark, any two):** 1. Detection of *Rapid Eye Movement (REM)* sleep in polysomnography. 2. Assessment of the Retinal Pigment Epithelium (RPE) — e.g., diagnosis of *Best Disease* (Arden Ratio test). 3. Eye-movement tracking in Human-Computer Interface (HCI) and assistive devices for the disabled. ### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]** *What is the Nernst Equation? Write the form used at body temperature (37 °C) and state any two applications.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** The Nernst Equation gives the equilibrium reduction potential of an electrochemical half-cell as a function of ion concentrations in the surrounding electrolyte. It is: $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q)$. - **Body-temperature form (1 mark):** At $T = 310 \text{ K}$, the equation simplifies to: $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{61.5}{n} \log_{10}(Q)$ mV. - **Applications (1 mark, any two):** 1. Predicts the half-cell potential of bio-potential electrodes such as Ag/AgCl and Calomel. 2. Calculates the equilibrium (Nernst) potential for ions across a cell membrane (e.g., $E_{K} \approx -90$ mV for potassium). 3. Foundation of the Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) equation for resting membrane potential. 4. Used to predict the open-circuit voltage of any two-electrode biopotential recording system. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Units mismatch** — Mixing mV with V, or omitting the `n` (charge number) when writing the Nernst Equation. Always state the value of $n$. > 2. **Logarithm base confusion** — Confusing $\ln$ (natural log) and $\log_{10}$. KTU paper uses the **base-10 logarithm at 37 °C** form (the 61.5 mV form) almost exclusively. > 3. **Forgetting the factor 2.303** — When converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ in derivations, students often forget the multiplier. > 4. **EOG/EGG amplitude vs. frequency mix-up** — EOG is in **mV (DC-10 Hz)**, EGG is in **μV (around 0.05 Hz)**. Confusing these is a guaranteed mark-loser. > 5. **No diagram in EOG/EGG questions** — Always draw a labelled block diagram of the recording setup; this is worth 2–3 marks by itself. > 6. **Microelectrode impedance** — Students often state the *amplifier input impedance* instead of the *electrode source impedance*. Be precise: microelectrode source impedance is **1–100 MΩ**. --- ### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle and recording technique of Electrooculography (EOG). Mention the typical signal amplitude, frequency range, and any four clinical applications. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the Nernst Equation from thermodynamic principles. Hence evaluate the Nernst potential for a potassium ion at body temperature, given $[K^+]_o = 5$ mM and $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question A — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Block Diagram (2 marks):** *Refer to the EOG block diagram in SECTION 4.1.* **Principle (2 marks):** - The human eye acts as a **fixed electric dipole**, with the cornea at a positive potential and the retina at a negative potential. - The *corneo-retinal standing potential* is **0.4 to 1.0 mV**. - When the eye moves, this dipole rotates, altering the voltage picked up by skin electrodes placed around the orbit. **Recording Technique (1 mark):** - Typically, **4 to 5 surface Ag/AgCl electrodes** are placed: one above and one below the eye (vertical EOG), and one lateral to each eye (horizontal EOG), with a reference on the forehead. - The differential signal is amplified by a high-CMRR instrumentation amplifier (CMRR > 100 dB). - Signal is band-pass filtered (**0.1 Hz – 30 Hz**) and digitised. **Signal Characteristics (1 mark):** - **Amplitude:** 0.5 to 1.0 mV. - **Frequency band:** DC to ~10 Hz. **Clinical Applications (1 mark, any four):** 1. REM-sleep detection in polysomnography. 2. Diagnosis of Best Vitelliform Macular Dystrophy. 3. Assessment of Retinal Pigment Epithelium (RPE) function. 4. Eye-movement tracking in HCI and gaming. 5. Diagnosis of ophthalmologic and neurological disorders (e.g., internuclear ophthalmoplegia). #### **Solution to Question A — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Derivation (5 marks):** *Step 1 — Write the general reversible half-reaction:* $$aA + bB + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$ *Step 2 — Gibbs Free Energy under non-standard conditions:* $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln(Q_r)$$ *Step 3 — Relate work to potential:* $$-\Delta G = nFE \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\Delta G^{\circ} = nFE^{\circ}$$ *Step 4 — Combine:* $$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln(Q_r)$$ *Step 5 — Isolate E:* $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q_r)$$ *Step 6 — Convert to base-10:* $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10}(Q_r)$$ *Step 7 — Evaluate the constant at T = 310 K:* $$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$ Thus: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{61.5}{n} \log_{10}(Q_r) \;\; \text{mV at 37 °C}$$ **Numerical Evaluation (2 marks):** *For the potassium equilibrium potential:* $$E_{K} = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}\right) \;\; \text{mV}$$ Substituting $[K^+]_o = 5$ mM and $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM: $$\begin{aligned} E_{K} &= 61.5 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{5}{140}\right) \;\; \text{mV} \\[4pt] &= 61.5 \times \log_{10}(0.0357) \;\; \text{mV} \\[4pt] &= 61.5 \times (-1.447) \;\; \text{mV} \\[4pt] &\approx -89.0 \;\; \text{mV} \end{aligned}$$ **Result:** $E_{K} \approx -89$ mV, which closely matches the known Nernst potential for K⁺ across a typical neuronal membrane. --- ### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the principle, recording setup, and clinical applications of Electrogastrography (EGG). Include a neat block diagram and mention the typical frequency and amplitude of the gastric slow wave. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of micropipette glass microelectrodes. Compare them with metal microelectrodes in terms of impedance, tip size, and applications. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B — Part (a) [7 marks]** **Block Diagram (2 marks):** *Refer to the EGG block diagram in SECTION 4.2.* **Principle (2 marks):** - The stomach's smooth muscle is driven by a *pacemaker* — the Interstitial Cells of Cajal (ICC) located in the upper body of the stomach near the greater curvature. - These pacemaker cells generate a *gastric slow wave* — a rhythmic electrical depolarisation that propagates toward the pylorus. - The EGG records this myoelectrical activity non-invasively using abdominal surface electrodes. **Recording Setup (1 mark):** - **3 to 4 Ag/AgCl electrodes** placed on the cleaned abdominal skin along the gastric axis. - A reference electrode is placed on a distant site. - The signal is amplified (~1500× gain), band-pass filtered (0.033 Hz – 0.25 Hz), and digitised. - *Running Spectral Analysis* (FFT of 1–4 min windows) extracts the dominant frequency. **Signal Characteristics (1 mark):** - **Normal slow-wave frequency:** 0.05 Hz (**3 cycles per minute**). - **Amplitude (surface):** 100 – 500 μV. **Clinical Applications (1 mark, any four):** 1. Diagnosis of gastroparesis and gastric motility disorders. 2. Detection of gastric dysrhythmia — bradygastria (< 2 cpm), tachygastria (4–9 cpm). 3. Nausea assessment in motion sickness, pregnancy, and chemotherapy. 4. Post-operative evaluation of gastric function. 5. Evaluation of functional dyspepsia and irritable bowel syndrome. #### **Solution to Question B — Part (b) [7 marks]** **Micropipette Glass Microelectrode — Construction (2 marks):** - A **borosilicate glass capillary** is heated and mechanically pulled to form two pipettes with very fine tips (tip diameter typically **0.5 – 5 μm**). - The tip is back-filled with an electrolyte solution, usually **3 M KCl** (because K⁺ has equal mobility with Cl⁻, minimising junction potentials). - A chlorided silver wire (Ag/AgCl) is inserted into the shaft to connect the electrolyte to the amplifier. - *Refer to the microelectrode block diagram in SECTION 4.4.* **Working (2 marks):** - The micropipette is mounted on a *micromanipulator* and advanced until the tip penetrates a single cell membrane. - The intracellular fluid (rich in K⁺) makes contact with the KCl electrolyte inside the tip, completing a low-impedance ionic pathway. - Voltage differences between the inside of the cell and the external reference electrode are amplified by a high-impedance buffer. - Used to measure *resting membrane potential* (typically **−70 mV**) and *action potentials* of single neurons or muscle cells. **Comparison with Metal Microelectrodes (3 marks):** | Parameter | Micropipette (Glass) | Metal (Tungsten/Pt-Ir) | |---|---|---| | **Tip Diameter** | 0.5 – 5 μm | 1 – 10 μm | | **Impedance at 1 kHz** | 10 – 100 MΩ | 0.5 – 5 MΩ | | **Recording Type** | Intracellular (single cell) | Extracellular (multi-unit) | | **Tip Potential** | ± 5 to ± 30 mV (drifts) | Negligible | | **Application** | Resting potential, ion-selective, patch clamp | Neural prosthetics, deep-brain stimulation, EMG | | **Reusability** | Single-use (fragile) | Limited reuse (can be re-etched) | --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (10-second scan before exam):** - **EOG** records the *corneo-retinal standing potential* of the eye using surface electrodes around the orbit. Cornea is **positive**, retina is **negative**. Amplitude: **0.4 – 1.0 mV**, Band: **DC – 10 Hz**. Use: REM-sleep detection, RPE assessment (Best Disease), HCI. - **EGG** records the *gastric slow wave* from the ICC pacemaker using surface electrodes on the abdomen. Normal frequency: **3 cpm (0.05 Hz)**. Amplitude: **100 – 500 μV**. Filter band: **0.033 – 0.25 Hz**. Use: gastroparesis, dysrhythmia, nausea. - **Nernst Equation (general):** $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q)$. - **Nernst Equation (37 °C, log base 10):** $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{61.5}{n} \log_{10}(Q)$ in **mV**. - **Constants to memorise:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96485$ C/mol, $T_{\text{body}} = 310$ K. - The factor **2.303** converts $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$. At 37 °C, the term $\frac{2.303 RT}{F} = 61.5$ mV. - The **Nernst potential for K⁺** with $[K^+]_o = 5$ mM and $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM is approximately **−89 mV** (this is a frequently asked numerical). - The **Helmholtz double layer** at the electrode-electrolyte interface is the basis of all bio-potential electrode behaviour. Capacitance: **10–40 μF/cm²**. - **Microelectrode** = ultra-fine electrode (tip **< 10 μm**) for single-cell recording. - **Metal microelectrodes** → low impedance (**0.5 – 5 MΩ**), extracellular recording. - **Micropipette (glass) microelectrodes** → high impedance (**10 – 100 MΩ**), intracellular recording, filled with **3 M KCl**. - Microelectrodes require a **high-input-impedance buffer** (> 10¹² Ω) — e.g., **AD549, OPA129, TL072** — placed physically close to the tip to prevent signal loss. - The **eye behaves as a fixed dipole**, but the **stomach behaves as a distributed pacemaker** — this is the key conceptual difference in their biopotential origins. - Always remember to **draw a block diagram** in any EOG/EGG answer (worth 2–3 marks on its own in KTU evaluation).

topics.title: Skin Surface Electrodes and Needle Electrodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET612 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Skin Surface Electrodes and Needle Electrodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Biopotential Electrode?

A **biopotential electrode** is a transducer that converts the ionic current flowing through a biological tissue (e.g., the heart, brain, or muscle) into an electronic current that can be measured, amplified, and recorded by an external instrument such as an ECG, EEG, or EMG machine.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, electrodes are classified as the **primary sensing interface** of any biomedical instrumentation system, forming the critical bridge between the biological domain (ions in electrolyte) and the electronic domain (electrons in metal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Electrodes are the *first* element in the signal-acquisition chain. Any error, artifact, or impedance mismatch introduced at the electrode level cannot be fully corrected later, no matter how sophisticated the amplifier.

## 1.2 Skin Surface Electrodes — Formal Definition

A **skin surface electrode** (also called a *surface electrode* or *cutaneous electrode*) is a biopotential electrode that is placed externally on the intact skin surface to pick up bioelectric signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG) without penetrating the skin barrier.

These electrodes work by establishing an **electrolyte bridge** between the conductive metal (e.g., Ag/AgCl) and the skin. A thin layer of **electrolyte gel** (typically NaCl or KCl saturated) is used to facilitate ionic conduction.

### Conceptual Analogy 🍎
> Imagine you are trying to "listen" to a conversation inside a closed room (your heart or brain). The **skin** is the thick concrete wall. A **surface electrode** is like sticking a **stethoscope** against the wall — you can hear a muffled version of what is happening inside, but the sound is dampened. A **needle electrode** is like drilling a tiny microphone *through* the wall and placing it directly inside the room — the signal is much clearer and more localized, but it is invasive.

## 1.3 Needle Electrodes — Formal Definition

A **needle electrode** is a biopotential electrode that is **inserted percutaneously** (through the skin) into the underlying muscle, nerve, or tissue to record bioelectric activity with high spatial resolution. The metallic conductor is coated with a thin insulating layer (e.g., Parylene, Teflon) except at the recording tip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Materials Used in Needle Electrodes:**
> - **Stainless Steel** (surgical grade 304/316) — cheap, but polarizable
> - **Platinum-Iridium (Pt-Ir, 90/10)** — inert, low noise, used in chronic implants
> - **Tungsten** — very fine tip, used in microelectrodes and neurology
> - **Ag/AgCl** — combined with electrolyte for non-polarizable behavior

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrode-skin impedance** | **1 kΩ to 100 kΩ** (at 1 Hz) | Lower is better for high-fidelity recording |
| **Half-cell potential** | **~+0.222 V** for Ag/AgCl | Should match between electrodes to cancel DC offset |
| **Polarization voltage** | < **±5 mV** for Ag/AgCl | Indicates non-polarizable behavior |
| **Frequency range** | **0.05 Hz – 1500 Hz** (clinical EMG) | Needle electrodes extend upper limit to ~10 kHz |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrode-Electrolyte Interface — Physics

At the heart of every electrode is a **charge-transfer interface** between an electronic conductor (metal) and an ionic conductor (electrolyte/tissue). When a metal M is immersed in its own ion solution, the reaction:

$$M \rightleftharpoons M^{n+} + ne^{-}$$

reaches a dynamic equilibrium, producing a **half-cell potential** (also called the *electrode potential*). This is the fundamental source of the DC offset that any biopotential amplifier must reject.

### 2.1.1 Nernst Equation — The Core Relationship

The equilibrium potential across the metal-electrolyte interface is given by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{a_{Ox}}{a_{Red}} \right)$$

Where:
- $E$ = Electrode (half-cell) potential in Volts
- $E^{0}$ = Standard electrode potential at **25 °C, 1 atm, 1 M activity**
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature in Kelvin (**310 K** for body temperature)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred per ion
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $a_{Ox}, a_{Red}$ = Chemical activities of oxidized and reduced species

### 2.1.2 Simplified Nernst at Body Temperature

At body temperature ($T = 310 \text{ K}$), and converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$:

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{0.0615}{n} \log_{10} \left( \frac{a_{Ox}}{a_{Red}} \right) \quad \text{(Volts, at 37°C)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** When a pair of identical electrodes is used (e.g., two Ag/AgCl electrodes), their half-cell potentials theoretically cancel out. In practice, slight mismatch produces a *DC offset voltage* (typically **±5 mV to ±100 mV**) that the instrumentation amplifier must reject via high **CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio)**.

## 2.2 Equivalent Circuit of the Electrode-Skin Interface

The surface electrode can be modeled as an **electrical network** consisting of:

1. **$E_{hc}$** — Half-cell potential (DC source)
2. **$R_d$** — Charge-transfer (Faradaic) resistance at metal-electrolyte interface
3. **$C_d$** — Double-layer capacitance at the metal-electrolyte interface
4. **$R_s$** — Series resistance of the electrolyte gel
5. **$R_e$, $C_e$** — Skin impedance (epidermis acts as RC network)
6. **$R_b$** — Body tissue bulk resistance (deep layer)

> [!NOTE]
> **Double Layer Capacitance ($C_d$):** At the metal-electrolyte boundary, charge separation occurs over a distance of ~1 nm, forming a **Helmholtz double layer** that behaves like a parallel-plate capacitor. Typical $C_d$ = **10 μF/cm² to 40 μF/cm²** for Ag/AgCl.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E^{0} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{a_{Ox}}{a_{Red}} \right)$ | Nernst half-cell potential | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $E_{37} = E^{0} + \dfrac{0.0615}{n} \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{a_{Ox}}{a_{Red}} \right)$ | Nernst at body temperature | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $Z_{e-skin} = R_{s} + \dfrac{R_{d}}{1 + j \omega R_{d} C_{d}}$ | Total electrode-skin impedance | Ohms (Ω) |
| 4 | $\tau = R_{d} C_{d}$ | Time constant of electrode interface | Seconds (s) |
| 5 | $V_{motion} = \Delta E_{hc} + \Delta (I \cdot Z_{e-skin})$ | Motion artifact voltage | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}} \right)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio | Decibels (dB) |
| 7 | $i_{d} = i_{0} \left[ e^{\frac{(1-\alpha) n F \eta}{RT}} - e^{\frac{-\alpha n F \eta}{RT}} \right]$ | Butler-Volmer current-overpotential | Amperes (A) |
| 8 | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{d} C_{d}}$ | High-pass corner frequency of electrode | Hertz (Hz) |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU LaTeX Note for Tables:** In the table above, the **vertical pipe symbol** inside the impedance expression was deliberately replaced with `\vert` rendering and parentheses. Using a raw `|` character inside a markdown table cell will **break the table parser** in the PDF/Markdown renderers used by KTU's online learning portal. Always use parentheses, `\vert`, or `\mid` for absolute value or modulus inside tables.

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

- **Nernst Equation:** Used to **select electrode pairs** with matched potentials. Mismatched potentials cause saturation of the front-end amplifier.
- **Impedance $Z_{e-skin}$:** Determines the **input impedance requirement** of the amplifier. Rule of thumb: amplifier input impedance must be **≥ 10× the source impedance** to avoid signal attenuation.
- **$R_d C_d$ Time constant:** Sets the **low-frequency response** of the recording. A large $\tau$ means the electrode can faithfully record slow signals (e.g., ECG baseline).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Simplified Skin-Electrode Impedance from Equivalent Circuit

### Step 1 — Start with the Basic Circuit Model
The series combination of half-cell potential $E_{hc}$ and the Faradaic branch (resistor $R_d$ in parallel with capacitor $C_d$) is connected in series with the electrolyte resistance $R_s$ and the skin RC network $(R_e \parallel C_e)$.

The total impedance seen by the amplifier, $Z_{total}$, is:

$$Z_{total} = R_{s} + Z_{interface} + Z_{skin}$$

### Step 2 — Write the Interface Impedance
The interface consists of $R_d$ in **parallel** with $C_d$:

$$Z_{interface} = \frac{R_{d} \cdot \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_{d}}}{R_{d} + \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_{d}}}$$

### Step 3 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $j \omega C_d$
To eliminate the complex fraction in the denominator:

$$Z_{interface} = \frac{R_{d}}{1 + j \omega R_{d} C_{d}}$$

**Explanation:** This is the standard form of a **parallel RC impedance**. The numerator $R_d$ is the **DC resistance**, and the denominator shows that impedance **decreases with frequency** because the capacitor shorts out $R_d$ at high frequencies.

### Step 4 — Simplify the Skin Impedance
The skin (specifically the **stratum corneum**, the outermost dead-cell layer) is also modeled as $R_e$ in parallel with $C_e$:

$$Z_{skin} = \frac{R_{e}}{1 + j \omega R_{e} C_{e}}$$

### Step 5 — Combine Everything
Substituting back into the original expression:

$$Z_{total} = R_{s} + \frac{R_{d}}{1 + j \omega R_{d} C_{d}} + \frac{R_{e}}{1 + j \omega R_{e} C_{e}}$$

### Step 6 — Magnitude of Impedance
Taking the magnitude of the interface term (the dominant contributor at low frequencies):

$$\vert Z_{interface} \vert = \frac{R_{d}}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_{d} C_{d})^{2}}}$$

**At very low frequencies** ($\omega \to 0$): $\vert Z_{interface} \vert \to R_d$ (max, dominates)
**At very high frequencies** ($\omega \to \infty$): $\vert Z_{interface} \vert \to 0$ (capacitor shorts out)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why **stratum corneum abrasion** or **gel penetration** dramatically lowers the impedance — by creating a low-resistance pathway around $R_e$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Motion Artifact Voltage

### Step 1 — Define the Cause
A motion artifact occurs when the **electrode moves relative to the skin**, disturbing the double-layer charge distribution.

### Step 2 — Express the Artifact Voltage
The artifact voltage is the sum of two components: (i) change in half-cell potential, and (ii) ohmic drop across variable impedance:

$$V_{motion}(t) = \Delta E_{hc}(t) + \Delta I(t) \cdot Z_{e-skin}(t)$$

### Step 3 — Approximate the Half-Cell Potential Change
The half-cell potential follows Nernst. A change in ion concentration $\Delta c$ at the interface produces:

$$\Delta E_{hc} = \frac{RT}{nF} \cdot \frac{\Delta c}{c_{0}}$$

### Step 4 — The Current is Galvanic
The motion-induced current $\Delta I$ arises from charge redistribution and is approximately:

$$\Delta I \approx C_{d} \cdot \frac{d(\Delta E_{hc})}{dt}$$

### Step 5 — Final Motion Artifact Expression
Combining:

$$V_{motion}(t) = \frac{RT}{nF} \cdot \frac{\Delta c}{c_{0}} + C_{d} \frac{d}{dt}\left( \frac{RT}{nF} \cdot \frac{\Delta c}{c_{0}} \right) \cdot Z_{e-skin}(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implication:** This is the **theoretical justification** for using **Ag/AgCl** electrodes — they exhibit *much smaller* $\Delta E_{hc}$ under motion because the half-cell reaction is **reversible** (non-polarizable), unlike pure silver or stainless steel.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Surface vs Needle Electrodes

| Parameter | Skin Surface Electrode | Needle Electrode |
|---|---|---|
| **Invasiveness** | Non-invasive | Invasive (percutaneous) |
| **Spatial Selectivity** | Poor (records from large tissue volume) | Excellent (records from few fibers) |
| **Signal Amplitude (EMG)** | **50 μV – 5 mV** | **100 μV – 10 mV** (higher SNR) |
| **Bandwidth** | **20 Hz – 500 Hz** | **20 Hz – 10 kHz** |
| **Pain / Discomfort** | None | Mild to moderate (patient discomfort) |
| **Risk of Infection** | Negligible | Requires sterilization (hepatitis, HIV risk) |
| **Motion Artifact** | High (especially with poor gel contact) | Low (firmly anchored in tissue) |
| **Skin Preparation Needed** | Yes (cleaning, abrasion) | Minimal (alcohol swab) |
| **Typical Application** | ECG, EEG, clinical EMG screening | Single-fiber EMG, nerve conduction, deep EEG |
| **Reusability** | Often disposable | Must be sterilized or disposable |
| **Cost** | Low (₹10–₹500 per piece) | Higher (₹100–₹5000) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Corner Frequency of Electrode Interface

The electrode interface behaves as a **first-order high-pass filter** due to $R_d C_d$ combination. The corner frequency (–3 dB point) is:

### Step 1 — Define Corner Frequency
At the corner frequency, the real and imaginary parts of $Z_{interface}$ are equal in magnitude.

### Step 2 — Write the Condition
$$\omega_{c} R_{d} = \frac{1}{\omega_{c} C_{d}}$$

### Step 3 — Solve
$$\omega_{c}^{2} = \frac{1}{R_{d} C_{d}}$$

$$\omega_{c} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{R_{d} C_{d}}}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Hz
$$f_{c} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{d} C_{d}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example:** For typical Ag/AgCl, $R_d \approx 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C_d \approx 1 \mu F$ (effective), giving:
> $$f_{c} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^{4} \times 10^{-6}} \approx 15.9 \text{ Hz}$$
> This means the electrode attenuates frequencies below ~16 Hz — the **baseline wander region of ECG**. The instrumentation amplifier's AC coupling is therefore essential.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Electrode-Skin Interface Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Metal Conductor M] --> B[Double Layer Capacitance Cd]
    A --> C[Charge Transfer Resistance Rd]
    B --> D[Electrolyte Gel Rs]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Stratum Corneum Re and Ce]
    E --> F[Subcutaneous Tissue Rb]
    F --> G[Amplifier Input]
    H[Half Cell Potential Ehc] --> A
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Current flows from the **metal conductor** through the **Faradaic interface** (Cd parallel to Rd) and the **electrolyte gel resistance Rs**, then through the **skin layer** (Re parallel to Ce) and finally into the deep tissue (Rb) before reaching the amplifier. The DC source **Ehc** represents the half-cell potential that must be rejected by the differential amplifier.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Biopotential Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Patient Body] --> E1[Surface Electrode 1]
    P1 --> E2[Surface Electrode 2]
    P1 --> E3[Reference Electrode RL]
    E1 --> IA[Differential Instrumentation Amplifier]
    E2 --> IA
    E3 --> IA
    IA --> HPF[High Pass Filter 0.05 Hz]
    HPF --> AMP[Main Amplifier Gain 1000x]
    AMP --> LPF[Low Pass Filter 1500 Hz]
    LPF --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> DSP[Digital Signal Processing]
    DSP --> DISP[Display and Storage]
```

> **Engineering Walkthrough:** The two active surface electrodes (E1, E2) feed the **differential** input of the instrumentation amplifier. The third electrode (RL) is the **driven right leg** reference (a KTU-favorite topic), which actively feeds back common-mode noise to cancel interference. The signal chain then proceeds through **AC coupling → Amplification → Anti-aliasing → ADC → DSP**.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Tree: Types of Biopotential Electrodes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Biopotential Electrodes] --> B[Surface Electrodes]
    A --> C[Needle Electrodes]
    A --> D[Microelectrodes]
    B --> B1[Pregelled Disposable]
    B --> B2[Reusable Plate]
    B --> B3[Suction Electrode]
    B --> B4[Floating Dry Electrode]
    C --> C1[Monopolar Needle]
    C --> C2[Concentric Bipolar Needle]
    C --> C3[Multielectrode Needle Array]
    C --> C4[Hooked Wire Electrode]
    D --> D1[Metal Microelectrode]
    D --> D2[Glass Micropipette]
    D --> D3[FET Based Microelectrode]
```

---

## 4.4 Cross-Section Schematic of a Surface Electrode on Skin

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Lead Wire Connection] --> M1[Ag or AgCl Metal Disk]
    M1 --> G1[Electrolyte Gel Layer]
    G1 --> S1[Stratum Corneum Dead Cells]
    S1 --> S2[Viable Epidermis]
    S2 --> S3[Dermis with Sweat Glands]
    S3 --> S4[Subcutaneous Fat and Muscle]
    S4 --> REF[Reference Electrode Path]
```

> **Note on Diagram Style:** Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, this is rendered as a **block-level functional architecture flow** rather than a literal anatomical cross-section, because Mermaid cannot faithfully reproduce biological tissue textures. The flow nonetheless correctly represents the **layer-by-layer signal path** from the lead wire down to the reference electrode site.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define half-cell potential. Why is Ag/AgCl preferred over pure silver for biopotential recording?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The **half-cell potential** is the electric potential developed at the interface between a metal and its electrolyte when the metal-ion exchange reaction reaches dynamic equilibrium. It is mathematically described by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{a_{Ox}}{a_{Red}} \right)$$

**Why Ag/AgCl is preferred (2 Marks):**
1. **Non-polarizable behavior:** Ag/AgCl exhibits a **reversible** electrochemical reaction $AgCl + e^{-} \rightleftharpoons Ag + Cl^{-}$. The current can cross the interface freely, so the half-cell potential remains stable under varying current.
2. **Low and stable offset voltage:** Polarization voltage is typically less than **±5 mV**, compared to hundreds of mV for pure Ag or stainless steel.
3. **Low noise:** Reduced motion artifact due to reversible reaction kinetics.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List any three advantages of needle electrodes over surface electrodes.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):**

1. **Higher Spatial Selectivity:** Needle electrodes record from a very small tissue volume (a few motor units), enabling single-fiber EMG and precise nerve conduction studies.
2. **Wider Frequency Bandwidth:** They capture high-frequency components up to **10 kHz**, essential for studying fast action potentials.
3. **Reduced Motion Artifact:** Being anchored in muscle tissue, they are immune to skin-electrode interface disturbances that plague surface electrodes.
4. *(Bonus)* **Better Signal-to-Noise Ratio** for deep signals such as deep EEG or laryngeal EMG.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the equivalent circuit model of the **electrode-electrolyte-skin interface**. Derive the expression for total impedance.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw and Label the Circuit (2 Marks)**

The equivalent circuit consists of:
- DC source $E_{hc}$ (half-cell potential) in series with
- A parallel combination of $R_d$ (charge transfer resistance) and $C_d$ (double-layer capacitance)
- Followed by series resistance $R_s$ of the electrolyte
- In series with $R_e$ in parallel with $C_e$ representing stratum corneum
- In series with bulk tissue resistance $R_b$

**Step 2 — Write the Interface Impedance (2 Marks)**

The Faradaic interface impedance is:

$$Z_{interface} = \frac{R_{d}}{1 + j \omega R_{d} C_{d}}$$

This is derived by taking the parallel combination of $R_d$ and the capacitor impedance $\frac{1}{j \omega C_d}$, then simplifying.

**Step 3 — Write the Skin Impedance (1 Mark)**

$$Z_{skin} = \frac{R_{e}}{1 + j \omega R_{e} C_{e}}$$

**Step 4 — Final Total Impedance (2 Marks)**

$$\boxed{Z_{total} = R_{s} + \frac{R_{d}}{1 + j \omega R_{d} C_{d}} + \frac{R_{e}}{1 + j \omega R_{e} C_{e}} + R_{b}}$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the construction, working principle, and clinical applications of **concentric bipolar needle electrodes** and **monopolar needle electrodes**.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Monopolar Needle | Concentric Bipolar Needle |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Single insulated needle shaft, tip exposed; requires separate **surface reference electrode** | Two conductors: an inner insulated wire (active) and the outer cannula (reference), separated by insulation |
| **Working Principle** | Records potential difference between the **needle tip** and a **distant surface electrode** | Records potential difference between the **inner core tip** and the **outer shaft** (both inside the muscle) |
| **Pickup Volume** | Larger (3–5 cm radius) | Smaller, more selective (~0.5–1 mm) |
| **Common Use** | Routine clinical EMG, nerve studies | Single-fiber EMG, motor unit potential analysis |
| **Signal Quality** | More susceptible to cross-talk from adjacent muscles | Excellent isolation; high spatial resolution |
| **Implant Procedure** | Single insertion | Single insertion but technically more complex to manufacture |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The concentric needle is preferred when **localization** is critical, while the monopolar needle is sufficient for **routine EMG screening** and is more economical.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Nernst Equation** in detail. A silver electrode is immersed in a solution of $AgNO_3$ with ion activity $a_{Ag^{+}} = 0.1$. Given $E^{0} = +0.799 \text{ V}$ for $Ag^{+}/Ag$ at 25 °C, calculate the half-cell potential.

**Model Solution:**

**Theoretical Explanation (4 Marks):**

The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium potential of an electrochemical half-cell:

$$E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{a_{Ox}}{a_{Red}} \right)$$

For the silver half-cell reaction: $Ag^{+} + e^{-} \rightleftharpoons Ag$, we have $n = 1$, $a_{Ox} = a_{Ag^{+}}$, and $a_{Red} = 1$ (pure solid).

**Step 1 — Convert Temperature**
$$T = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values into Nernst Equation**
$$E = 0.799 + \frac{(8.314)(298)}{(1)(96485)} \ln(0.1)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Thermal Voltage Term**
$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.02569 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Logarithm**
$$\ln(0.1) = -2.3026$$

**Step 5 — Multiply**
$$E = 0.799 + (0.02569)(-2.3026) = 0.799 - 0.0592$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer (3 Marks)**
$$\boxed{E = +0.7398 \text{ V} \approx +0.740 \text{ V (vs. Standard Hydrogen Electrode)}}$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction of a **floating dry electrode** and list its advantages over conventional pregelled electrodes.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
A floating dry electrode is designed such that the metal contact does **not directly press** against the skin. The architecture is:
- **Outer plastic housing** with adhesive backing to attach to the skin.
- **Inner cavity** filled with **electrolyte gel** (trapped inside).
- **Metal snap** (Ag/AgCl) suspended in the gel, connected to the lead wire.
- The skin-electrode mechanical contact is decoupled: mechanical pressure on the housing is **not transmitted** to the metal-skin interface, hence the name "**floating**".

**Working Principle (1 Mark):**
The skin-electrode electrical interface (gel contact) is **isolated** from external mechanical disturbances, dramatically reducing motion artifact.

**Advantages (3 Marks — 1 Each):**

1. **Drastically reduced motion artifact** because the gel-skin interface is not disturbed by movement.
2. **Faster application** — no skin abrasion or gel spreading required; can be applied in seconds.
3. **Suitable for long-term monitoring** (Holter ECG, ambulatory EEG) because the electrolyte reservoir prevents gel drying.
4. *(Bonus)* **Better patient comfort** due to soft, compliant housing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the Nernst equation:** Students often write the final answer without explicitly stating "Volts" or "vs. SHE". This costs **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 2. **Wrong number of electrons ($n$):** For $Ag^{+}/Ag$, $n = 1$. For $Cu^{2+}/Cu$, $n = 2$. Confusing these gives wrong answers.
> 3. **Mixing up monopolar vs concentric:** Many students describe monopolar as having "two electrodes inside" — wrong. Monopolar has only ONE active tip.
> 4. **Not drawing $E_{hc}$ in the equivalent circuit:** The DC offset source must be shown, otherwise the diagram is incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Treating the skin as pure resistance:** The stratum corneum is **capacitive** at low frequencies; forgetting $C_e$ is a frequent deduction point.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ An **electrode** is a **transducer** converting ionic current to electronic current.
- ✅ **Half-cell potential** is given by the **Nernst Equation**; $E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{a_{Ox}}{a_{Red}} \right)$.
- ✅ At body temperature (37 °C), the Nernst slope is **0.0615/n V per decade**.
- ✅ **Ag/AgCl** is the **gold standard** biopotential electrode due to its **non-polarizable** nature.
- ✅ The electrode-skin interface has **three key layers**: Faradaic interface, electrolyte gel, and stratum corneum.
- ✅ Impedance **decreases with frequency** because $C_d$ and $C_e$ short out their parallel resistors.
- ✅ **Surface electrodes** are non-invasive but have **poor spatial selectivity** and high motion artifact.
- ✅ **Needle electrodes** are invasive but provide **excellent spatial resolution** and **wider bandwidth** (up to 10 kHz).
- ✅ **Monopolar needle** needs an external surface reference; **concentric bipolar** has the reference built into the needle shaft.
- ✅ **Floating dry electrodes** decouple the mechanical and electrical contact, eliminating motion artifact.
- ✅ **Corner frequency** of electrode interface: $f_{c} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{d} C_{d}}$.
- ✅ **CMRR of the front-end amplifier** must be > 80 dB to reject electrode mismatch voltages.
- ✅ **Polarization** = irreversible charge transfer (avoid); **Non-polarization** = reversible reaction (Ag/AgCl, preferred).
- ✅ **Double-layer capacitance** arises from charge separation at the metal-electrolyte interface (Helmholtz layer).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Electro Conduction System of the Heart. Electrocardiography - ECG Lead Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET612 - BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Overview of Electro Conduction System of the Heart. Electrocardiography - ECG Lead Configurations ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 The Cardiac Electrical Conduction System — Formal Definition The **Electro Conduction System of the Heart** is a specialised network of autorhythmic cardiac muscle cells responsible for the **spontaneous generation, ordered propagation, and coordinated distribution of electrical impulses** through the myocardium. This intrinsic system ensures that the atria contract first (top-down), followed by the ventricles (bottom-up), producing a synchronised mechanical pump action that is essential for efficient blood circulation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The conduction system is the *electrical foundation* of every ECG waveform you will ever interpret. Without understanding the *origin* and *pathway* of the impulse, the P-QRS-T morphology on the ECG paper has no meaning. **The five sequential components are:** 1. **Sinoatrial (SA) Node** — Primary Pacemaker 2. **Atrioventricular (AV) Node** — Delay Station 3. **Bundle of His** — Conduction Bridge 4. **Right and Left Bundle Branches** — Distributors 5. **Purkinje Fibre Network** — Terminal Activator ### 1.2 Electrocardiography (ECG/EKG) — Formal Definition **Electrocardiography** is the non-invasive biomedical recording technique that captures the **summed electrical activity of the cardiac cycle** from the body surface using skin electrodes. The resultant tracing — the **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** — is a voltage-versus-time plot representing the depolarisation and repolarisation phases of the myocardium. Standard clinical parameters (must be memorised): - **Paper speed = 25 mm/s** - **Vertical calibration = 10 mm/mV** - **1 small box = 1 mm = 0.04 s** and **0.1 mV** - **1 large box = 5 mm = 0.20 s** and **0.5 mV** - **Normal heart rate (adult) = 60–100 beats per minute (bpm)** ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "The Conductor and the Orchestra"** > Imagine the heart as a 100-piece orchestra. The **SA Node** is the *lead conductor* — it waves the baton (fires the impulse) at a tempo of ~70 bpm. The **AV Node** is the *gatekeeper between rehearsals* — it briefly holds musicians back (~0.1 s delay) so the string section (atria) finishes *before* the brass section (ventricles) joins in. The **Bundle of His, Bundle Branches, and Purkinje Fibres** are the *walkways and amplifiers* that carry the conductor's signal instantly to every musician. The **ECG machine** is the *recording microphone placed in the auditorium* — it does not make the music, it simply captures the sound waves (voltage deflections) reaching the surface. ### 1.4 KTU Board Exam Tip > [!IMPORTANT] > For a 14-mark question on the conduction system, the examiner expects you to mention *autorhythmicity*, the **intrinsic rate of each component**, and the **direction of impulse flow**. Merely listing five names fetches only partial marks. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Architecture of the Cardiac Conduction System The cardiac muscle has a unique property called **Autorhythmicity** (or Automaticity) — the ability of certain specialised cells to spontaneously depolarise and reach threshold without external neural stimulus. The intrinsic firing rates form a *descending hierarchy*: $$\text{SA Node (70–80 bpm)} > \text{AV Node (40–60 bpm)} > \text{Bundle of His (40 bpm)} > \text{Purkinje Fibres (20–40 bpm)}$$ Because the SA Node fires the fastest, it **overrides** all lower pacemakers — a phenomenon called **"Overdrive Suppression"**. This is why the SA Node is the *normal pacemaker* of the heart. #### Step-by-Step Operational Logic **Stage 1 — SA Node Firing (Right Atrium, near SVC opening)** - Specialised *P cells* undergo spontaneous **Phase 4 slow diastolic depolarisation**. - Slow Na⁺ "funny" current (I_f) + Ca²⁺ influx → threshold → rapid depolarisation. - Frequency ≈ **70–80 impulses/minute**. Wave spreads across both atria → produces the **P wave** on ECG. **Stage 2 — Internodal Pathways & AV Node Delay** - Impulse travels via *Anterior, Middle, and Posterior Internodal Tracts* to the AV Node. - AV Node cells are small, slow-conducting. Conduction velocity drops to **0.02–0.05 m/s**. - This **0.1 second delay** allows complete atrial emptying (atrial kick) before ventricular contraction. Segment on ECG: **P–Q (PR) interval**. **Stage 3 — Bundle of His (AV Bundle)** - The only electrical bridge between atria and ventricles (the fibrous skeleton insulates the rest). - Enters the interventricular septum and splits into two branches. **Stage 4 — Right and Left Bundle Branches** - **Right Bundle Branch (RBB):** Runs along the right side of the septum → right ventricle. - **Left Bundle Branch (LBB):** Splits into *anterior fascicle* and *posterior fascicle* → left ventricle. - Conduction velocity here is high (~2–4 m/s) to allow rapid, synchronised ventricular activation. **Stage 5 — Purkinje Fibre Network** - Spread across the endocardial surface of the ventricles. - Very high conduction velocity (~4 m/s) — fastest in the heart. - Triggers ventricular myocardium from apex → base (bottom-up squeeze for efficient ejection). - Produces the **QRS complex** on ECG. > [!NOTE] > The **ST segment** represents the *plateau phase* (Phase 2) of ventricular myocyte action potential — all cells are depolarised, so no net voltage difference exists on the body surface. The **T wave** represents ventricular *repolarisation* (Phase 3). ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Parameter** | **Equation / Value** | **Units** | **Notes** | |---|---|---|---| | Heart Rate (small-box method) | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{1500}{N_{\text{small}}}$ | bpm | $N_{\text{small}}$ = small squares between two consecutive R peaks | | Heart Rate (large-box method) | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{300}{N_{\text{large}}}$ | bpm | $N_{\text{large}}$ = large squares between R peaks | | Heart Rate (6-second method) | $\text{HR} = (\text{R waves in 30 large boxes}) \times 2$ | bpm | Used for irregular rhythms | | Time per small box | $t = 0.04$ | s | At 25 mm/s paper speed | | Time per large box | $T = 0.20$ | s | At 25 mm/s paper speed | | Voltage per small box (vertical) | $V = 0.1$ | mV | At 10 mm/mV calibration | | Voltage per large box (vertical) | $V = 0.5$ | mV | At 10 mm/mV calibration | | **Einthoven's Law** | $I + III = II$ | dimensionless (lead vectors) | Fundamental KCL-equivalent law of ECG | | Cardiac Output | $CO = HR \times SV$ | L/min | Where $SV$ = stroke volume | | Mean Electrical Axis (frontal plane) | $\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{\sum QRS_{aVF}}{\sum QRS_{\text{I}}}\right)$ | degrees | Normal axis: −30° to +90° | ### 2.3 ECG Lead Configurations — Theoretical Foundation A **Lead** is *not* a single wire — it is a *viewpoint* of the heart's electrical activity from a specific spatial angle. The standard clinical **12-lead ECG** actually uses only **10 electrodes** to construct 12 different "views." **Three Categories of Leads:** | **Category** | **Number** | **Reference** | **Type** | |---|---|---|---| | Bipolar Limb Leads | 3 (I, II, III) | Einthoven (1913) | Measures potential *between* two limb electrodes | | Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads | 3 (aVR, aVL, aVF) | Goldberger (1942) | Measures potential at one limb *w.r.t.* a reference terminal | | Unipolar Precordial (Chest) Leads | 6 (V1–V6) | Wilson (1934) | Measures potential at chest points *w.r.t.* Wilson Central Terminal | #### 2.3.1 Bipolar Limb Leads (Einthoven's Triangle) Three electrodes form an **equilateral triangle** (Einthoven's Triangle) on the body: - **RA (Right Arm)** — placed on right forearm - **LA (Left Arm)** — placed on left forearm - **LL (Left Leg)** — placed on left calf (or ankle) **Lead I** = $V_{LA} - V_{RA}$ (records lateral heart, left arm positive) **Lead II** = $V_{LL} - V_{RA}$ (records inferior heart, left leg positive — the *most commonly monitored* lead in ICU/OT) **Lead III** = $V_{LL} - V_{LA}$ (records inferior heart) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Einthoven's Law** (derived from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law): $I + III = II$. This must hold true on every normal ECG and is a quick self-check for lead-misplacement errors. KTU has asked direct questions on this law — memorise it. #### 2.3.2 Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger) These are modified Wilson leads. The exploring electrode is on one limb, and the *other two limb electrodes* are connected through resistors to form the reference. This *augments* the signal amplitude by **50%** (gain factor = 1.5), hence the prefix **"a"**. - **aVR** — Augmented Vector Right: $V_{aVR} = -\dfrac{I + II}{2}$ - **aVL** — Augmented Vector Left: $V_{aVL} = \dfrac{I - III}{2}$ - **aVF** — Augmented Vector Foot: $V_{aVF} = \dfrac{II + III}{2}$ #### 2.3.3 Precordial (Chest) Leads (Wilson) Six electrodes are placed on the chest at anatomically defined points: | **Lead** | **Position** | **Cardiac Region Viewed** | |---|---|---| | V1 | 4th intercostal space, right sternal border | Septal / Right ventricle | | V2 | 4th intercostal space, left sternal border | Septal / Right ventricle | | V3 | Midway between V2 and V4 | Anterior wall | | V4 | 5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line | Apex / Anterior wall | | V5 | Same level as V4, anterior axillary line | Lateral wall | | V6 | Same level as V4, mid-axillary line | Lateral wall | **Reference terminal:** Wilson Central Terminal (WCT) = average of RA, LA, LL potentials (≈ 0 mV reference). ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering > [!NOTE] > The biomedical engineer's role is to: > 1. Design **high-CMRR instrumentation amplifiers** (> 100 dB) to reject 50/60 Hz mains interference and baseline wander. > 2. Implement **digital filters** (notch filter at 50/60 Hz, 0.5 Hz high-pass for baseline). > 3. Ensure **patient isolation** (IEC 60601-1) using optocouplers/transformers to prevent microshock. > 4. Develop **lead-failure detection algorithms** (using Einthoven's Law as a real-time check). > 5. Build **12-lead simultaneous sampling systems** (≥ 1000 Hz sampling rate per channel). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Einthoven's Law **Starting Point:** Einthoven's Triangle is an *equilateral triangle* with the heart at its centroid. The three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) form the three vertices. Let $V_{RA}$, $V_{LA}$, $V_{LL}$ be the potentials at the three limb electrodes. **Step 1 — Define the three bipolar lead voltages (using KVL sign convention):** $$V_{\text{I}} = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$$ $$V_{\text{II}} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$ $$V_{\text{III}} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$ **Step 2 — Add Lead I and Lead III algebraically:** $$V_{\text{I}} + V_{\text{III}} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})$$ **Step 3 — Cancel the common term $V_{LA}$ (algebraic simplification):** $$V_{\text{I}} + V_{\text{III}} = V_{LA} - V_{RA} + V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$ $$V_{\text{I}} + V_{\text{III}} = - V_{RA} + V_{LL}$$ $$V_{\text{I}} + V_{\text{III}} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$ **Step 4 — Recognise the right-hand side as Lead II:** $$V_{\text{I}} + V_{\text{III}} = V_{\text{II}}$$ $$\boxed{\;V_{\text{I}} + V_{\text{III}} = V_{\text{II}}\;}$$ > **Interpretation:** This is a direct consequence of **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** applied to the closed loop of the body. Any violation of this law on a recorded ECG is a *red flag* for lead misplacement, electrode failure, or measurement noise. ### 3.2 Derivation of Heart Rate from R-R Interval **Given:** Standard paper speed $v = 25$ mm/s, and $N$ small squares between two consecutive R peaks. **Step 1 — The horizontal distance between R peaks on paper:** $$d = N \times 1 \text{ mm} = N \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — The time interval between R peaks (R-R interval):** $$T_{RR} = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{N \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ mm/s}} = \dfrac{N}{25} \text{ seconds}$$ **Step 3 — Heart rate is the inverse of the cardiac period:** $$HR = \dfrac{1}{T_{RR}} \text{ beats/second}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to beats per minute (multiply by 60):** $$HR = \dfrac{1}{T_{RR}} \times 60 = \dfrac{60 \times 25}{N} = \dfrac{1500}{N} \text{ bpm}$$ $$\boxed{\;HR = \dfrac{1500}{N_{\text{small squares}}}\; \text{bpm}}$$ **Numerical Example:** If $N = 20$ small squares between two R peaks: $$HR = \dfrac{1500}{20} = 75 \text{ bpm (Normal)}$$ **Numerical Example 2:** If $N = 10$ small squares: $$HR = \dfrac{1500}{10} = 150 \text{ bpm (Tachycardia)}$$ **Numerical Example 3:** If $N = 30$ small squares: $$HR = \dfrac{1500}{30} = 50 \text{ bpm (Bradycardia)}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Cardiac Output from ECG-Derived HR Cardiac Output cannot be obtained from ECG alone — ECG provides only the *electrical* HR, while Stroke Volume (SV) must come from echocardiography. However, the *electrical* HR is a critical input: **Step 1 — Definition of Cardiac Output (CO):** $$CO = HR \times SV$$ **Step 2 — Substitute ECG-derived HR expression:** $$CO = \left(\dfrac{1500}{N}\right) \times SV \text{ mL/min}$$ **Step 3 — Convert to Litres per minute:** $$CO = \dfrac{1500 \times SV}{1000 \times N} = \dfrac{1.5 \times SV}{N} \text{ L/min}$$ **Worked Example:** $N = 25$ small squares, $SV = 70$ mL: $$HR = \dfrac{1500}{25} = 60 \text{ bpm}$$ $$CO = 60 \times 70 = 4200 \text{ mL/min} = 4.2 \text{ L/min (Normal)}$$ ### 3.4 Derivation of Mean Electrical Axis (Hexaxial Reference System) The mean electrical axis is the *average direction* of ventricular depolarisation in the frontal plane, measured in degrees from the horizontal (Lead I = 0°). **Step 1 — Sum the QRS deflections (R wave − S wave, in mm) in Leads I and aVF:** $$\sum QRS_{\text{I}} = R_{\text{I}} -
