# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 4.1 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) System

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** is a non-invasive diagnostic imaging modality that exploits the nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) phenomenon of hydrogen protons (¹H) in body tissues to produce high-resolution, multi-planar, cross-sectional images based on the spatial encoding of radiofrequency (RF) signals emitted during proton relaxation.

**Expanded Academic Definition:**
MRI relies on the alignment of nuclear spins (primarily hydrogen nuclei abundant in water and fat) within a strong static magnetic field (typically **0.5 T to 3 T**, with research systems up to **7 T**), excitation of these spins using resonant RF pulses (Larmor frequency: **42.58 MHz/T**), and detection of the resulting free induction decay (FID) signal as the spins relax back to equilibrium.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Spinning Top in a Magnetic Field Analogy:**
> Imagine billions of tiny spinning tops (hydrogen protons) inside your body. Normally, they spin in random directions. When you place them inside a very powerful magnet (the MRI bore), most of them align with the magnetic field — like compass needles pointing North. A brief radio "knock" (RF pulse) tips them sideways. As they wobble back into alignment, they emit faint radio signals. By carefully listening to these signals from different locations, the MRI computer reconstructs a detailed map of where the hydrogen is concentrated — and thus, where the different tissues (brain, muscle, fat) are located.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Gyromagnetic ratio (γ) for ¹H | **42.58 MHz/T** | Larmor frequency constant |
| Typical static field (B₀) | **0.5 T – 3 T** | Clinical MRI range |
| RF excitation frequency | **8.5 – 128 MHz** | Matches Larmor frequency |
| T1 relaxation (gray matter) | **~1300 ms** | Longitudinal relaxation |
| T2 relaxation (gray matter) | **~90 ms** | Transverse relaxation |
| T1 relaxation (CSF) | **~3000 ms** | Fluid is bright on T1 with contrast |
| T2 relaxation (CSF) | **~2000 ms** | Fluid is bright on T2-weighted |

---

## 4.2 Nuclear Medicine System

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Nuclear Medicine Imaging** is a branch of medical imaging that uses unsealed radioactive sources (radiopharmaceuticals) administered to the patient, and detects the gamma rays (or positrons) emitted from within the body to produce functional/metabolic images of organs and physiological processes, in contrast to the anatomical imaging of MRI/CT.

**Two principal modalities:**
1. **Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography (SPECT)** — uses gamma-emitting isotopes (e.g., **Tc-99m**, energy **140 keV**).
2. **Positron Emission Tomography (PET)** — uses positron-emitting isotopes (e.g., **F-18 FDG**, energy **511 keV** annihilation photons).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Glowing Sugar Cube Analogy:**
> Think of giving a patient a tiny amount of radioactive "sugar" (FDG for PET). Cancer cells love sugar and gobble it up. As the radioactive sugar decays inside the body, it emits invisible "flashes of light" (gamma rays). A special camera (the gamma/PET camera) placed around the patient acts like a 3D flashlight detector, mapping exactly where the flashes come from. This reveals not just what the organ *looks like* (anatomy) but what it is *doing* (function — metabolism, blood flow).

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Isotope | Modality | Half-life | Energy | Clinical Use |
|---------|----------|-----------|--------|--------------|
| **Tc-99m** | SPECT | **6 hours** | 140 keV | Bone, heart, thyroid scans |
| **I-131** | SPECT/therapy | 8.02 days | 364 keV | Thyroid |
| **F-18 (FDG)** | PET | **110 min** | 511 keV | Oncology, neurology |
| **Tl-201** | SPECT | 73 hours | 70–80 keV | Myocardial perfusion |
| **Ga-67** | SPECT | 78 hours | 93–296 keV | Infection, lymphoma |

---

## 4.3 Ultrasonic Imaging System — Introduction and Basic Principle

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagnostic Ultrasonography** is a non-invasive, real-time imaging modality that uses high-frequency mechanical sound waves (typically **2–15 MHz**, well above human hearing threshold of **20 kHz**) transmitted into the body via a piezoelectric transducer. The reflected echoes from tissue interfaces are processed to construct a grayscale anatomical image based on acoustic impedance differences.

**Core principle:** **Pulse-Echo Principle** — the same transducer acts as both transmitter and receiver. Time-of-flight of the echo determines depth; amplitude of the echo determines brightness.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bats-and-Caves Analogy / Submarine SONAR Analogy:**
> Bats fly through dark caves emitting high-pitched squeaks and listen for the echoes to "see" the cave walls. Submarines use SONAR to map the ocean floor. Medical ultrasound does the same inside the human body: a probe sends out high-frequency sound "chirps," and the echoes bouncing back from different tissues (blood, muscle, bone) are timed to create a 2D picture. The deeper a structure, the longer the echo takes to return.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value | Note |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Diagnostic frequency range | **2–15 MHz** | Higher freq = better res, less penetration |
| Speed of sound in soft tissue (c) | **1540 m/s** | Used for depth calculation |
| Average acoustic impedance (Z) of soft tissue | **1.6 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)** | Z = ρ × c |
| Acoustic impedance of bone | **7.8 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)** | Large mismatch → strong reflection |
| Acoustic impedance of air | **~0.0004 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)** | Massive mismatch |
| Wavelength (5 MHz) | **0.308 mm** | Determines axial resolution |
| Penetration depth (3.5 MHz) | ~15–20 cm | Abdominal imaging |
| Penetration depth (7.5 MHz) | ~5 cm | Vascular/thyroid |

---

## 4.4 Colour Doppler Systems

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Colour Doppler Imaging** is a specialised ultrasound technique that overlays a colour-coded map of blood flow velocity and direction on top of a real-time B-mode grayscale anatomical image. It uses the **Doppler shift** — the change in frequency of ultrasound waves reflected from moving red blood cells (RBCs) — to estimate flow velocity, with the **autocorrelation technique** providing real-time, two-dimensional velocity mapping.

**Colour Convention (Most Systems):**
- **Red** = flow **toward** the transducer
- **Blue** = flow **away from** the transducer
- **Lighter shades** = higher velocities
- **Darker shades** = lower velocities

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Siren on a Fire Engine Analogy:**
> You've heard a fire engine's siren change pitch as it approaches (higher pitch) and moves away (lower pitch). That pitch change is the Doppler effect. Colour Doppler works identically with sound waves inside the body. When blood flows toward the probe, the reflected waves return at a higher frequency (shown RED — "warm/hot" toward you). When blood flows away, frequency drops (shown BLUE — "cold" moving away). The faster the flow, the brighter the shade.

### Doppler Equation (Key Formula)

$$\boxed{f_d = \frac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}}$$

Where:
- $f_d$ = Doppler frequency shift (Hz)
- $f_0$ = Transmitted frequency (Hz)
- $v$ = Blood flow velocity (m/s)
- $\theta$ = Angle between ultrasound beam and flow direction
- $c$ = Speed of sound in tissue (**1540 m/s**)

> [!WARNING]
> The cosine term means that at $\theta = 90°$ the Doppler shift is **zero** (no flow detected). Always angle the beam at **30°–60°** to the vessel for accurate measurement.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MRI — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step)

1. **Patient placement inside B₀ magnet:** All hydrogen proton spins align either parallel (low energy) or anti-parallel (high energy) to B₀. Net magnetization vector (NMV) aligns **along +Z axis**.

2. **RF excitation (Tipping pulse):** A 90° RF pulse at Larmor frequency flips the NMV into the **X-Y (transverse) plane**, creating transverse magnetization $M_{xy}$.

3. **Free Precession:** The spins precess around B₀ at the Larmor frequency $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$.

4. **T1 Relaxation (Longitudinal / Spin-Lattice):** Spins release energy to the surrounding lattice and return to +Z alignment. Characteristic time **T1** is the time for $M_z$ to recover **63%** of its equilibrium value.

5. **T2 Relaxation (Transverse / Spin-Spin):** Spins dephase in the X-Y plane due to magnetic field interactions with neighbors. Characteristic time **T2** is the time for $M_{xy}$ to decay to **37%** of its initial value.

6. **Spatial Encoding using Gradient Coils:**
   - **Slice-select gradient (G_ss):** Selects a 2D slice.
   - **Phase-encoding gradient (G_pe):** Phase-encodes rows.
   - **Frequency-encoding (readout) gradient (G_fe):** Encodes columns by frequency.

7. **Signal acquisition:** Free induction decay (FID) is captured by RF receiver coils.

8. **Image reconstruction:** 2D or 3D **Inverse Fourier Transform** of k-space data produces the MR image.

### KTU Formula Sheet — MRI

| Formula | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---------|-----------|------------------|
| **Larmor Equation** | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | Precession frequency |
| **Larmor frequency (linear)** | $f_0 = 42.58 \times B_0 \text{ MHz}$ | At 1 T: 42.58 MHz |
| **Resonance condition** | $f_{RF} = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ | RF must match precession |
| **T1 contrast** | $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$ | Longitudinal recovery |
| **T2 contrast** | $M_{xy}(t) = M_{0,xy} \cdot e^{-t/T_2}$ | Transverse decay |
| **SNR (approx.)** | $\text{SNR} \propto B_0 \cdot V \cdot \sqrt{N_{acq}}$ | Higher field → better SNR |
| **Spatial resolution** | $\Delta x = \frac{FOV}{N_{matrix}}$ | Pixel size |
| **Field gradient relation** | $\Delta f = \gamma \cdot G \cdot \Delta x$ | Frequency encoding |

### Real-World Engineering Utility of MRI
- Detection of tumors, stroke, multiple sclerosis.
- Functional MRI (fMRI) — maps brain activity via BOLD (Blood Oxygen Level Dependent) effect.
- Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (MRS) — chemical analysis of metabolites.
- Interventional MRI for image-guided surgery.

---

## 2.2 Nuclear Medicine — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### Operational Sequence (SPECT/PET)

1. **Radiopharmaceutical administration:** Intravenous injection of tracer (e.g., **Tc-99m MDP** for bone, **F-18 FDG** for metabolism).

2. **Tracer uptake:** Physiological uptake occurs in target organ (e.g., FDG in glucose-avid tumors; MDP in active bone metabolism).

3. **Radioactive decay:** Isotope emits gamma ray (SPECT) or positron (PET).

4. **SPECT Detection:** Gamma photons (140 keV) detected by a **NaI(Tl) scintillation crystal** coupled to PMTs. A **collimator** (parallel-hole, fan-beam, or cone-beam) ensures only photons travelling along defined paths are accepted.

5. **PET Detection:** Positron annihilates with electron, producing **two 511 keV photons at 180°** (collinear). Coincidence detection (electronic collimation) localises the annihilation event along the line of response (LOR).

6. **Image reconstruction:**
   - SPECT: **Filtered Back Projection (FBP)** or iterative methods (OSEM).
   - PET: **Iterative reconstruction** (maximum likelihood expectation maximisation, MLEM) — required due to attenuation and scatter.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Nuclear Medicine

| Formula | Expression | Meaning |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| **Activity (A)** | $A = \lambda N$ | $\lambda$ = decay constant |
| **Decay law** | $A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential decay |
| **Half-life** | $T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$ | Time to halve activity |
| **Mean life** | $\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{T_{1/2}}{0.693}$ | Average lifetime |
| **Spatial resolution (PET)** | $\Delta x \approx 4–6 \text{ mm}$ | Limited by positron range |
| **SUV (Standardised Uptake Value)** | $SUV = \frac{\text{Activity in ROI}}{\text{Injected dose / body weight}}$ | Quantification in PET |
| **Spatial resolution (SPECT)** | $\Delta x \approx 8–10 \text{ mm}$ | Limited by collimator |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- Oncology staging (FDG-PET/CT).
- Cardiac perfusion imaging (Tc-99m sestamibi SPECT).
- Thyroid function and ablation therapy (I-131).
- Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease diagnosis (FDG-PET, DaTscan SPECT).
- Hybrid imaging: **SPECT/CT, PET/CT, PET/MRI**.

---

## 2.3 Ultrasonic Imaging — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### Operational Sequence (Pulse-Echo)

1. **Pulse generation:** A high-voltage pulse (50–200 V) is applied to the piezoelectric crystal at a **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** of **1–10 kHz**.

2. **Transmission:** The crystal vibrates at its resonant frequency, producing a short ultrasound pulse (~2–4 cycles).

3. **Propagation through tissue:** Beam attenuates via:
   - **Absorption** (energy → heat; dominant mechanism)
   - **Scattering** (Rayleigh scattering from RBCs)
   - **Reflection** at impedance boundaries

4. **Reflection at tissue interfaces:** Reflection coefficient:
$$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

5. **Echo detection:** Returning echoes vibrate the crystal, generating voltage signals.

6. **Image formation:**
   - **A-mode (Amplitude):** Spike display, time-of-flight on X-axis, amplitude on Y.
   - **B-mode (Brightness):** 2D grayscale image; brightness ∝ echo amplitude.
   - **M-mode (Motion):** Time-motion display for cardiac valves.

7. **Depth calculation:** 
$$\text{Depth} = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} = \frac{1540 \times t}{2} \text{ (m)}$$

### KTU Formula Sheet — Ultrasound

| Formula | Expression | Meaning |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| **Acoustic impedance** | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | $\rho$ = density, $c$ = speed of sound |
| **Reflection coefficient (intensity)** | $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Fraction of intensity reflected |
| **Transmission coefficient** | $T = 1 - R$ | Fraction transmitted |
| **Depth from echo time** | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Round-trip time |
| **Wavelength** | $\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$ | Determines resolution |
| **Axial resolution** | $\Delta z = \frac{\text{Pulse length}}{2} = \frac{n \lambda}{2}$ | Better at high freq |
| **Lateral resolution** | $\Delta x \approx \lambda \cdot \frac{z}{D}$ | Depends on beam width |
| **Attenuation coefficient** | $\alpha(f) \approx 0.5 \text{ dB/cm/MHz (soft tissue)}$ | Frequency dependent |
| **Total attenuation** | $A = \alpha \cdot f \cdot d$ (dB) | Two-way = 2× |
| **PRF constraint (depth)** | $d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$ | Max unambiguous depth |
| **SNR** | $\text{SNR} \propto \text{freq}^{1.5}$ | Higher freq → better SNR (limited by depth) |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- Obstetrics (fetal imaging), Cardiology (echocardiography), Radiology (abdominal), Vascular (Doppler), Emergency (FAST scans), and image-guided interventions.

---

## 2.4 Colour Doppler — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### Operational Steps

1. **Transmission:** Multiple pulses along each scan line (typically 8–16 pulses per line).

2. **Echo reception & phase detection:** For each sample volume, the phase shift between successive echoes is measured.

3. **Autocorrelation processing:** The mean phase shift (mean velocity) and variance (turbulence/energy) are computed for each pixel.

4. **Colour map generation:** The mean velocity is colour-coded:
   - **Red** → positive (toward transducer)
   - **Blue** → negative (away from transducer)
   - **Hue** → velocity magnitude
   - **Saturation** → degree of turbulence (Power Doppler variant)

5. **B-mode overlay:** Greyscale anatomy displayed simultaneously, with colour flow superimposed.

6. **Aliasing avoidance:** PRF must be at least **2× the maximum Doppler shift** (Nyquist limit). When exceeded, colour wraps around (e.g., high-velocity forward flow appears as reverse — aliased).

### KTU Formula Sheet — Doppler

| Formula | Expression | Meaning |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| **Doppler shift (general)** | $f_d = f_r - f_0$ | Shift between received & transmitted |
| **Doppler equation (US)** | $f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | Standard clinical Doppler |
| **Velocity from shift** | $v = \frac{c \cdot f_d}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | Solved for velocity |
| **Nyquist limit** | $f_{d,max} = \frac{\text{PRF}}{2}$ | Max shift without aliasing |
| **Maximum velocity (no alias)** | $v_{max} = \frac{c \cdot \text{PRF}}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | Upper limit of colour map |
| **Cosine correction** | $v_{true} = \frac{v_{measured}}{\cos\theta}$ | Corrects for beam angle |
| **Wall filter cutoff** | typically **50–200 Hz** | Removes low-velocity tissue motion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Wave (CW) vs Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler:**
> - **CW Doppler:** Two crystals (transmit + receive continuously). **No aliasing** but no depth discrimination.
> - **PW Doppler:** Single crystal, range-gated. Has depth specificity but subject to aliasing at high velocities.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- Detection of vascular stenosis, regurgitation (mitral, aortic), DVT, carotid artery disease, fetal heart assessment, tumor vascularity.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Larmor Equation (MRI)

### Step 1: Starting point — Torque on a magnetic moment

A proton with magnetic moment $\vec{\mu}$ placed in a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ experiences a torque:

$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}$$

**Conversion logic:** Torque equals the rate of change of angular momentum $\vec{L}$.

### Step 2: Equating torque to time rate of change of angular momentum

$$\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}$$

Since $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$ (where $\gamma$ is the gyromagnetic ratio), we substitute:

$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the fundamental equation of motion for a magnetic moment in a field.

### Step 3: Assume static field along Z-axis

Let $\vec{B} = B_0 \hat{z}$. Then expanding the cross product:

$$\frac{d\mu_x}{dt} = \gamma \mu_y B_0$$

$$\frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma \mu_x B_0$$

$$\frac{d\mu_z}{dt} = 0$$

**Conversion logic:** The Z-component is constant (no precession along field), while X and Y oscillate.

### Step 4: Solve the coupled ODEs

Differentiating the first equation again:

$$\frac{d^2\mu_x}{dt^2} = \gamma B_0 \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = -\gamma^2 B_0^2 \mu_x$$

This is the simple harmonic oscillator equation with solution:

$$\mu_x(t) = \mu_{\perp} \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$$

$$\mu_y(t) = -\mu_{\perp} \sin(\omega_0 t + \phi)$$

with the angular frequency:

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \gamma B_0}$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the Larmor precession frequency.

### Step 5: For hydrogen (¹H), $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8 \text{ rad/s/T}$

At $B_0 = 1.5 \text{ T}$:

$$f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi} = \frac{2.675 \times 10^8 \times 1.5}{2\pi} = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

This lies in the radiofrequency range — hence RF excitation is used.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Acoustic Reflection Coefficient

### Step 1: Boundary conditions at an interface

At a boundary between medium 1 and medium 2, three conditions must hold:
- **Pressure continuity:** $p_1 = p_2$ at $x = 0$.
- **Particle velocity continuity:** $u_1 = u_2$ at $x = 0$.

### Step 2: Express incident, reflected, transmitted waves

For a wave normally incident on the boundary:

$$p_i = P_i e^{j(\omega t - k_1 x)}$$

$$p_r = P_r e^{j(\omega t + k_1 x)}$$

$$p_t = P_t e^{j(\omega t - k_2 x)}$$

**Conversion logic:** Three waves; the incident travels toward the interface, reflected travels back, transmitted continues forward.

### Step 3: Apply continuity at $x = 0$

$$P_i + P_r = P_t$$

For velocity, $u = p / Z$:

$$\frac{P_i - P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_t}{Z_2}$$

**Conversion logic:** The negative sign accounts for the reversed direction of the reflected wave's particle velocity.

### Step 4: Solve the linear system

Adding the two equations weighted by $Z_1$ and $Z_2$:

$$P_r = P_i \cdot \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

$$P_t = P_i \cdot \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

### Step 5: Intensity (power) reflection coefficient

Since intensity ∝ $P^2 / Z$:

$$\boxed{R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2}$$

**Conversion logic:** This explains why the **fat–muscle interface** (mismatch small, $R \approx 0.01$) is dim, while the **soft tissue–bone interface** ($R \approx 0.4$) is bright.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Doppler Equation for Ultrasound

### Step 1: Moving observer (RBC) and stationary source

Consider a stationary transducer emitting frequency $f_0$ and an RBC moving with velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$ to the beam. The RBC "observes" a frequency:

$$f' = f_0 + \frac{v \cos\theta}{\lambda_0} = f_0 + \frac{f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

**Conversion logic:** The RBC moves *into* the wavefront, so it encounters waves at a higher rate.

### Step 2: The RBC then acts as a moving source

The wave reflected back toward the transducer is re-emitted at $f'$. The stationary transducer observes:

$$f_r = f' + \frac{v \cos\theta}{\lambda'} = f' + \frac{f' v \cos\theta}{c}$$

**Conversion logic:** The transducer is "behind" the receding wavefront, so it receives a lower frequency if RBC is moving away, higher if approaching.

### Step 3: Compute the Doppler shift

$$f_d = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c + v \cos\theta}$$

For $v \ll c$ (true clinically, where $v < 5 \text{ m/s}$ and $c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$):

$$\boxed{f_d \approx \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}}$$

**Conversion logic:** The denominator simplifies because $v \cos\theta \ll c$, giving the standard clinical formula.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Blood flows in a carotid artery at 0.5 m/s at an angle of 30° to a 5 MHz Doppler beam. Calculate the Doppler shift. (c = 1540 m/s)

**Solution:**

$$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot (5 \times 10^6) \cdot 0.5 \cdot \cos 30°}{1540}$$

$$\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$

$$f_d = \frac{2 \cdot 5 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.866}{1540} = \frac{4.33 \times 10^6}{1540} \approx 2812 \text{ Hz}$$

**Answer:** $f_d \approx 2.81 \text{ kHz}$ — within the audible range, hence Doppler signals are often played through speakers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Imaging Modalities

| Parameter | MRI | Nuclear Med (PET) | Nuclear Med (SPECT) | Ultrasound | Colour Doppler |
|-----------|-----|--------------------|--------------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Source** | RF + B₀ | F-18 (β⁺) | Tc-99m (γ) | Acoustic waves | Acoustic + shift |
| **Energy/Strength** | 42.58 MHz/T | 511 keV | 140 keV | 2–15 MHz | 2–15 MHz |
| **Image type** | Anatomic | Functional | Functional | Anatomic | Flow + anatomic |
| **Spatial resolution** | 0.5–2 mm | 4–6 mm | 8–10 mm | 0.5–2 mm | 1–2 mm |
| **Acquisition time** | 20–60 min | 20–40 min | 15–30 min | Real-time | Real-time |
| **Radiation** | None (non-ionising) | Yes (high) | Yes (moderate) | None | None |
| **Cost (₹/USD)** | Very high | High | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| **Cost (lakhs ₹)** | 500–1500 | 400–1000 | 200–500 | 5–50 | 15–50 |
| **Best use** | Brain, joints, soft tissue | Cancer staging | Bone, heart | OB/GYN, abdomen | Vascular, cardiac |
| **Limitations** | Long time, pacemakers | Cost, radiation | Lower resolution | Bone, air blocks | Angle dependency |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MRI System — Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0[Patient on Table] --> P1[Positioning inside bore]
    P1 --> P2[Static Magnet B0 0.5 to 3 T]
    P2 --> P3[Shim Coils for field uniformity]
    P3 --> P4[Gradient Coils X Y Z]
    P4 --> P5[RF Transmit Coil]
    P5 --> P6[RF Excitation Pulse at Larmor Frequency]
    P6 --> P7[Hydrogen proton spins tip into XY plane]
    P7 --> P8[Free Induction Decay signal emitted]
    P8 --> P9[RF Receive Coils surface array]
    P9 --> P10[Pre-amplifier and filtering]
    P10 --> P11[Analog to Digital Converter]
    P11 --> P12[FFT and K-space filling]
    P12 --> P13[Inverse Fourier Transform]
    P13 --> P14[Image reconstruction]
    P14 --> P15[Display on monitor]
    P15 --> P16[Console Computer for control and storage]
    P16 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram maps the functional architecture of a clinical MRI system from magnet to display. Each block represents a hardware subsystem or signal processing stage. The feedback loop back to positioning reflects the iterative nature of multi-slice acquisitions (T1, T2, FLAIR, etc.).

---

## 4.2 Nuclear Medicine (PET) — Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Patient Preparation Fasting Hydration] --> A1[IV Injection of F-18 FDG]
    A1 --> A2[Uptake Period 60 minutes]
    A2 --> A3[Patient on PET scanner bed]
    A3 --> A4[Annihilation Event Positron plus Electron]
    A4 --> A5[Two 511 keV Photons emitted 180 degrees]
    A5 --> A6[Detector Ring Scintillator crystals]
    A6 --> A7[Photomultiplier Tubes]
    A7 --> A8[Coincidence Detection Circuit]
    A8 --> A9[Line of Response LOR mapping]
    A9 --> A10[Attenuation Correction]
    A10 --> A11[Iterative Reconstruction OSEM MLEM]
    A11 --> A12[3D Functional Image]
    A12 --> A13[Fused with CT for anatomy]
    A13 --> A14[Display for clinical reading]
```

> [!NOTE]
> PET relies on coincidence detection to provide "electronic collimation" — eliminating the need for physical collimators. The 180°-collinear photon pair is detected within a timing window (~6–12 ns).

---

## 4.3 Ultrasonic Imaging — Pulse-Echo Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Pulser HV pulse generator] --> B1[Piezoelectric Transducer]
    B1 --> B2[Body Tissue acoustic impedance variations]
    B2 --> B3[Echoes return to transducer]
    B3 --> B4[TGC Time Gain Compensation]
    B4 --> B5[Amplifier and Envelope Detection]
    B5 --> B6[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    B6 --> B7[Scan Conversion to Cartesian]
    B7 --> B8[Grayscale Mapping]
    B8 --> B9[Display Monitor B mode]
    B1 -.reflected signal.-> B4
    B0 -.PRF trigger.-> B1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TGC (Time Gain Compensation):** Because deeper echoes are weaker (attenuation $\alpha \cdot f \cdot d$), the receiver gain is increased with time (depth) to produce a uniform-appearing image. This is essentially depth-dependent amplification.

---

## 4.4 Colour Doppler — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C0[PRF Pulse Train transmitted 8 to 16 pulses per line] --> C1[Echoes from moving RBCs]
    C1 --> C2[Phase shift between successive echoes detected]
    C2 --> C3[Autocorrelation Processor]
    C3 --> C4[Mean velocity calculation]
    C3 --> C5[Variance Turbulence calculation]
    C4 --> C6[Colour Map red toward blue away]
    C5 --> C6
    C6 --> C7[Wall Filter removes low velocity tissue motion]
    C7 --> C8[Combined with B mode anatomical image]
    C8 --> C9[Display on monitor]
    C10[Parallel B mode path] --> C8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Wall Filter (Clutter Filter):** Eliminates low-frequency, high-amplitude signals from slow-moving tissue (e.g., vessel walls), preserving only higher-frequency shifts from blood cells. Typical cutoff: **50–200 Hz**.

---

## 4.5 Concept Map — Relationships Between Modalities

```mermaid
graph TD
    M[Medical Imaging Modalities] --> M1[Anatomical Imaging]
    M --> M2[Functional Imaging]
    M --> M3[Hybrid Imaging]
    M1 --> M1a[MRI Soft tissue detail]
    M1 --> M1b[Ultrasound Real time safe]
    M1 --> M1c[CT X ray based]
    M2 --> M2a[PET Metabolism]
    M2 --> M2b[SPECT Perfusion]
    M2 --> M2c[Functional MRI BOLD]
    M3 --> M3a[PET CT]
    M3 --> M3b[SPECT CT]
    M3 --> M3c[PET MRI]
    M1b --> M1b1[Colour Doppler for vascular flow]
    M1a --> M1a1[MRA MR Angiography vascular without contrast]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

### Q1. Define Larmor frequency. What is its value for hydrogen in a 1.5 T MRI system?

**Model Answer:**

The **Larmor frequency** is the precession frequency of a magnetic moment (such as a proton's spin) in an external static magnetic field $B_0$. It is given by the Larmor equation:

$$f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

For hydrogen (¹H), $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8 \text{ rad/s/T}$, so at $B_0 = 1.5 \text{ T}$:

$$f_0 = \frac{2.675 \times 10^8 \times 1.5}{2\pi} = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

It is the frequency that the RF excitation pulse must match to achieve resonance in MRI. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

### Q2. What is acoustic impedance? Why is coupling gel used in ultrasound imaging?

**Model Answer:**

**Acoustic impedance** ($Z$) is the product of tissue density ($\rho$) and the speed of sound in that tissue ($c$):

$$Z = \rho \cdot c$$

It determines how much sound is reflected or transmitted at tissue interfaces. For soft tissue, $Z \approx 1.6 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$; for air, $Z \approx 0.0004 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$.

**Coupling gel** is used to eliminate the air gap between the transducer and skin. Without gel, the huge impedance mismatch between air and skin would reflect nearly **99.99%** of the ultrasound, preventing the sound from entering the body. The gel has $Z$ close to skin, allowing efficient transmission. (3 marks)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the principle of Magnetic Resonance Imaging. With a neat block diagram, describe the major subsystems of an MRI system. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss T1 and T2 relaxation mechanisms. Explain how T1-weighted and T2-weighted images differ in clinical interpretation, giving examples. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — MRI Principle & Subsystems (7 marks):**

**Principle (4 marks):**
1. **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance:** Hydrogen protons (¹H) possess a net magnetic moment due to their spin. In the absence of an external field, they orient randomly.
2. **Magnetisation:** When placed in a strong static field $B_0$ (along +Z), a slight excess of protons aligns with $B_0$, creating a net magnetisation vector $M_0$ along +Z.
3. **Excitation:** A 90° RF pulse at the Larmor frequency $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ tips $M_0$ into the transverse (XY) plane.
4. **Relaxation & Signal:** As the spins relax, they emit RF energy detected as the Free Induction Decay (FID). The decay envelope is governed by T1 (longitudinal recovery) and T2 (transverse dephasing).
5. **Spatial encoding:** Gradient coils (slice-select, phase-encoding, frequency-encoding) encode spatial position into the frequency/phase of the received signal.
6. **Reconstruction:** The collected data populates k-space; 2D Inverse Fourier Transform produces the image.

**Subsystems (3 marks):**
- **Main magnet** (superconducting, typically 1.5 T or 3 T) — provides $B_0$.
- **Gradient coils** (X, Y, Z) — provide spatial encoding.
- **RF transmit coil** — delivers excitation pulse.
- **RF receive coils** — detect the FID signal.
- **Shim coils** — correct $B_0$ inhomogeneities.
- **Computer & console** — controls pulse sequences, reconstructs images, displays results.
- **Patient table & bore** — housing and patient positioning.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — T1 and T2 Relaxation (7 marks):**

**T1 (Spin-Lattice / Longitudinal) Relaxation (3 marks):**
- Mechanism: Excited protons transfer energy to the surrounding molecular lattice (spin-lattice interaction).
- Time constant T1: Time for longitudinal magnetisation $M_z$ to recover to **63%** of $M_0$.
- Mathematical form: $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$
- T1 depends on tissue type: **Fat has short T1** (~250 ms at 1.5 T); **water/CSF has long T1** (~3000 ms).
- **T1-weighted images:** Use short TR (< T1) and short TE. **Fat appears bright** (white), water appears dark. Used for anatomical detail.

**T2 (Spin-Spin / Transverse) Relaxation (4 marks):**
- Mechanism: Spins dephase due to local magnetic field variations caused by neighbouring spins.
- Time constant T2: Time for transverse magnetisation $M_{xy}$ to decay to **37%** of its initial value.
- Mathematical form: $M_{xy}(t) = M_{0,xy} e^{-t/T_2}$
- T2 contrast: **Fluids (CSF, urine) have long T2** → appear **bright** on T2-weighted images. Pathological oedema and inflammation also appear bright.
- **T2-weighted images:** Use long TR and long TE. Excellent for detecting pathology (edema, inflammation, multiple sclerosis plaques).
- **Clinical example:** In stroke, T2-weighted images show bright infarcted regions within hours. In MS, T2 hyperintense lesions in white matter indicate demyelination.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the principles of SPECT and PET imaging. Compare their spatial resolution, sensitivity, and clinical applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of the Doppler equation, derive the relationship between Doppler shift and blood flow velocity. Discuss aliasing and methods to avoid it. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — SPECT vs PET (7 marks):**

**SPECT — Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography (3 marks):**
- Uses **gamma-emitting radiopharmaceuticals** (e.g., Tc-99m, 140 keV).
- Patient receives IV injection; tracer localises in target organ.
- **Gamma camera** (Anger camera) with NaI(Tl) crystal and PMT array detects emitted gamma rays.
- A **parallel-hole collimator** physically rejects photons not travelling along the axial direction (mechanical collimation).
- Multiple 2D projections acquired as the camera rotates around the patient.
- Reconstruction by **Filtered Back Projection (FBP)** or iterative methods (OSEM).
- Spatial resolution: **8–10 mm**.

**PET — Positron Emission Tomography (3 marks):**
- Uses **positron-emitting radiopharmaceuticals** (e.g., F-18 FDG, 110 min half-life).
- Positron annihilates with nearby electron → **two 511 keV gamma photons emitted 180° apart**.
- **Coincidence detection** in a ring of detectors — only simultaneous pairs are accepted (electronic collimation).
- Reconstruction by **iterative methods** (MLEM/OSEM) with attenuation correction.
- Spatial resolution: **4–6 mm** (limited by positron range before annihilation).

**Comparison (1 mark):**
| Parameter | SPECT | PET |
|-----------|-------|-----|
| Sensitivity | Lower | ~10–100× higher |
| Resolution | 8–10 mm | 4–6 mm |
| Isotopes | Tc-99m, I-131, Tl-201 | F-18, C-11, N-13 |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (cyclotron needed) |
| Clinical use | Bone scan, cardiac, thyroid | Oncology, neurology |

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Doppler Equation & Aliasing (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):** (As in Section 3.3)

For an RBC moving with velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$ to the beam:

$$f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

**Solving for velocity:**

$$\boxed{v = \frac{c \cdot f_d}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}}$$

**Aliasing (3 marks):**
- Pulse-Doppler systems sample at the **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)**.
- By the **Nyquist sampling theorem**, the maximum detectable Doppler shift is:
$$f_{d, max} = \frac{\text{PRF}}{2}$$
- When the true Doppler shift exceeds PRF/2, the system **underestimates** it and "wraps around" — e.g., a positive high-velocity shift appears as negative. This is called **aliasing** (analogous to the wagon-wheel effect in film).
- **Methods to avoid aliasing:**
  1. **Increase PRF** (decrease depth of interest).
  2. **Shift the baseline** of the colour map.
  3. **Use a lower transmit frequency** $f_0$.
  4. **Use Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler** — has no PRF limit and no aliasing, but lacks depth discrimination.
  5. **Steer the beam** to a smaller angle $\theta$ (reduces $f_d$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (MRI):** Students often confuse T1 and T2 — remember **T1 is recovery (longitudinal)**, **T2 is decay (transverse)**. In T1-weighted images, **fat is BRIGHT**; in T2-weighted images, **water/CSF is BRIGHT**.
> 
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (Doppler):** Many students forget the **cos θ** factor in the Doppler equation. **At θ = 90°, no flow is detected** (cos 90° = 0). Always state the **beam-to-vessel angle** in the answer.
> 
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (Nuclear Medicine):** Conflating **SPECT (gamma, 140 keV, physical collimator)** with **PET (positron, 511 keV, coincidence detection)**. Examiner specifically tests this distinction.
> 
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (Ultrasound):** Confusing **axial vs lateral resolution**. Axial resolution ≈ pulse length / 2 = (n·λ)/2. Lateral resolution depends on beam width.
> 
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Forgetting the **factor of 2** in the ultrasound Doppler equation (round trip). The CT/medical Doppler formula has 2; pure radar Doppler has only 1.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🧠 MRI Quick-Recall
- **Larmor frequency:** $f_0 = 42.58 \times B_0$ MHz (for ¹H).
- **T1 = longitudinal recovery** (energy to lattice); **T2 = transverse decay** (spin dephasing).
- **Spatial encoding:** Slice-select, Phase-encoding, Frequency-encoding gradients.
- **Image from:** 2D Inverse Fourier Transform of k-space.
- **No ionising radiation**, but **pacemakers are contraindicated**.

### 🧠 Nuclear Medicine Quick-Recall
- **SPECT:** Single gamma photon (e.g., Tc-99m, 140 keV), **physical collimator**.
- **PET:** Two coincident 511 keV photons (e.g., F-18 FDG), **electronic collimation**.
- **Reconstruction:** SPECT → FBP/OSEM; PET → iterative with attenuation correction.
- **Key isotopes:** Tc-99m (6 h), I-131 (8 d), F-18 (110 min), Tl-201 (73 h).
- **Clinical use:** Functional/metabolic imaging (oncology, cardiac, neurology).

### 🧠 Ultrasound Quick-Recall
- **Frequency:** 2–15 MHz; **c** = 1540 m/s in soft tissue.
- **Depth:** $d = ct/2$ (round-trip).
- **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$; **Reflection coefficient:** $R = [(Z_2-Z_1)/(Z_2+Z_1)]^2$.
- **Modes:** A (amplitude spike), B (2D grayscale), M (motion over time).
- **Coupling gel** essential to remove air-tissue impedance mismatch.
- **Axial resolution** ∝ 1/frequency; **penetration** ∝ 1/frequency.

### 🧠 Colour Doppler Quick-Recall
- **Equation:** $f_d = 2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c$.
- **Red** = toward transducer; **Blue** = away (BART convention: **B**lue **A**way, **R**ed **T**oward).
- **Nyquist limit:** $f_{d, max} = \text{PRF}/2$.
- **Wall filter** removes tissue motion artefact.
- **Best beam angle:** **30°–60°** to vessel (cos θ between 0.5 and 0.87).
- **Aliasing** in high-velocity jets (e.g., stenotic valves) — overcome by CW Doppler or higher PRF.

### 🔑 Critical Constants to Memorise
| Constant | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Gyromagnetic ratio ¹H | **42.58 MHz/T** |
| Speed of sound in tissue | **1540 m/s** |
| Tc-99m photon energy | **140 keV** |
| F-18 annihilation photon | **511 keV** |
| Human hearing threshold | **20 kHz** (US is above this) |
| Half-life of Tc-99m | **6 hours** |
| Half-life of F-18 | **110 minutes** |
